def_en: (British informal) drunk; intoxicated
def_zh: get squiffy, a bit squiffy
quote_en: "Sheila: 'You're squiffy.' Eric: 'I'm not.' Mrs Birling: 'What an expression, Sheila! Really the things you girls pick up these days!'" – During the family engagement dinner, Eric laughs unexpectedly and Sheila accuses him of being drunk; Mrs Birling immediately chides her language (Act 1)
quote_zh: Priestley's choice of the slang term 'squiffy' rather than 'drunk' introduces a note of unrefined reality into the Birling's polished celebration. The word belongs to the younger generation's informal speech, and its sudden appearance cracks the veneer of middle-class respectability. Eric's flat denial, followed by Mrs Birling's concern for propriety rather than for her son's actual state, exposes the family's habit of glossing over uncomfortable truths. The exchange foreshadows Eric's later confession of heavy drinking and his sexual encounter with Eva Smith, revealing that the Birlings' outward harmony rests on a foundation of secrets and avoidances that the Inspector is about to dismantle.
ex_en: Eric's squiffy state at the engagement dinner was an early warning of the hidden drinking that would entangle him in Eva Smith's tragedy.
ex_zh: The truth, squiffy with denial, stumbled about the dining room, knocking over the fragile ornaments of the family's self-esteem.

def_en: the rank, title, or status of a knight, an honour awarded by the monarch
def_zh: receive a knighthood, honours list, aspire to a knighthood
quote_en: "Birling: 'there's a fair chance that I might find my way into the next honours list. Just a knighthood, of course.' Gerald: 'Oh - I say - congratulations!' Birling: 'Thanks, but it's a bit too early for that. So don't say anything.'" – In confidential conversation after the ladies have left the table, Birling reveals his social ambitions to Gerald (Act 1)
quote_zh: 'Knighthood' in Birling's speech functions as a symbol of his materialism and social climbing. The phrase 'Just a knighthood, of course' mimics modesty while actually betraying his intense craving for status. His caution – 'don't say anything' – is rich with dramatic irony, because the Inspector's revelations will soon threaten the very 'good behaviour' on which such honours depend. Priestley uses the knighthood to crystallise the older generation's superficial values: respectability bought by wealth and connections, not earned through moral worth. Birling's obsession with public reputation blinds him to his private responsibilities, and the word becomes a marker of the hollow priorities that the play condemns.
ex_en: Birling's preoccupation with his knighthood revealed that he valued a public title far above private integrity, a failure the Inspector ruthlessly exposed.
ex_zh: The knighthood glittered in his mind like a polished brass nameplate, while the tarnish of his deeds crept invisibly over the letters.

def_en: easily influenced by others; open to being shaped or affected by experiences
def_zh: young and impressionable, impressionable age, highly impressionable
quote_en: "Mrs Birling: 'You seem to have made a great impression on this child, inspector.' Inspector: 'We often do on the young ones. They're more impressionable.'" – After Sheila has been deeply affected by the Inspector's questioning, Mrs Birling attempts to dismiss her reaction; the Inspector turns the remark into a statement about generational difference (Act 2)
quote_zh: Priestley loads 'impressionable' with immense thematic weight. Mrs Birling uses the word condescendingly, implying that Sheila's distress is childish weakness, but the Inspector reclaims it as a moral capacity. For him, being impressionable means being open to truth, guilt, and change – qualities entirely absent in the rigid older generation. The plural 'young ones' extends the idea beyond the Birling family to the whole generation that will inherit post-war Britain, aligning with Priestley's socialist hope that youth might learn the lessons of collective responsibility. The contrast between Mrs Birling's sneer and the Inspector's calm certainty dramatises the play's central conflict between entrenched prejudice and the possibility of redemption.
ex_en: Because Sheila and Eric were still impressionable, they could accept their share of guilt while their parents remained locked in self-justification.
ex_zh: Grief pressed into their impressionable hearts like a seal into warm wax, leaving a mark that no passage of time could wholly smooth away.

def_en: having or showing a strong and unreasoning desire for revenge
def_zh: vindictive streak, vindictive behaviour, feel vindictive
quote_en: "Gerald: 'You've been through it – and now you want to see somebody else put through it.' Sheila: 'so that's what you think I'm like. I'm glad I realized it in time, Gerald. … you've made up your mind I must obviously be a selfish, vindictive creature.'" – After Sheila's confession about Milwards, Gerald accuses her of wanting to see him suffer, and she fires back, naming the worst motive he might ascribe to her (Act 2)
quote_zh: 'Vindictive' becomes a flashpoint in Sheila's moral awakening. Gerald's accusation forces her to confront not only his hypocrisy but her own earlier cruelty at the shop. By saying aloud the word she fears he is thinking, Sheila drags both of them into an honest reckoning; she acknowledges that her actions against Eva Smith were born of jealousy and spite. Priestley places this adjective in the middle of a quarrel that shatters their engagement, symbolising the death of the cosy pre-Inspector world. The word ricochets between the lovers, marking the moment when easy romance gives way to painful truth and aligning Sheila with the Inspector's demand for unflinching self-examination.
ex_en: Sheila's recognition of her own vindictive impulse forced her to admit that her treatment of Eva Smith had been, however unconsciously, a form of revenge against her own unhappiness.
ex_zh: Vindictive as a late frost that blackens the first buds of spring, the memory of the insult crept back to wither every apology before it could bloom.

def_en: having or showing a dislike or distrust of someone based on preconceived opinion rather than reason
def_zh: prejudiced against, deeply prejudiced, prejudiced view
quote_en: "Inspector: 'you admit being prejudiced against her case?' Mrs Birling: 'Yes.' Sheila: 'mother, she's just died a horrible death – don't forget.'" – The Inspector confronts Mrs Birling about her refusal to help Eva Smith, who had appealed to the Brumley Women's Charity Organisation; Mrs Birling admits her bias without shame (Act 2)
quote_zh: Priestley sharpens his social critique through the word 'prejudiced'. The Inspector uses the language of a courtroom to pin Mrs Birling down, and her unhesitating 'Yes' is one of the most damning moments in the play. She treats her class bias as a principle, refusing to see beyond the girl's supposed impertinence. By making her prejudice explicit, the Inspector exposes the cruelty of an institutional charity that judges rather than helps. Sheila's immediate cry – reminding her mother of the girl's horrible death – pits emotional truth against cold self-righteousness. The exchange crystallises the play's central argument: a society governed by such prejudice is not simply unkind but lethal.
ex_en: Mrs Birling's confessed prejudice showed that the cruellest rejections can wear the mask of duty and respectability.
ex_zh: Prejudice settled in the room like a thin coat of dust on fine furniture, invisible to those who lived there but starkly obvious to any visitor who dared to wipe a finger across the surface.

def_en: a place in a large institution for the care of the sick or injured; a hospital
def_zh: taken to the infirmary, died in the infirmary, workhouse infirmary
quote_en: "Inspector: Two hours ago a young woman died on the infirmary. She'd been taken there this afternoon because she'd swallowed a lot of strong disinfectant. Burnt her inside out, of course. Eric: (involuntarily) My god! Inspector: Yes, she was in great agony. They did everything they could for her at the infirmary, but she died. Suicide, of course." – Inspector Goole announces the death of Eva Smith to the Birling family and Gerald Croft immediately upon his arrival, shattering their celebration (Act 1)
quote_zh: Priestley's repetition of the word 'infirmary'—cold, institutional, devoid of comfort—establishes the play's moral landscape. The infirmary is not a home but a place where the destitute go to die, invisible to families like the Birlings. By coupling it with the graphic "Burnt her inside out", Priestley strips away any euphemism, forcing the audience to confront the physical reality of Eva's suicide. Eric's involuntary "My god!" cracks the dinner-party complacency; the word 'infirmary' thus functions as an intrusion of harsh social truth into a world of port and cigars. It is the first note of the guilt that will bind all five characters together.
ex_en: Eva Smith's death in a public infirmary, far from the comfort of the Birling's home, symbolised the vast distance between the privileged and the desperate they claimed not to see.
ex_zh: The word 'infirmary' hung in the warm dining room like a sudden draught from a mortuary slab, chilling the champagne in their glasses.

def_en: intrusively enthusiastic in offering help or advice; interfering
def_zh: officious manner, officious meddling, consider officious
quote_en: "Birling: (angrily) Look here, inspector, I consider this uncalled- for and officious. I've half a mind to report you. I've told you all I know - and it doesn't seem to me very important - and now there isn't the slightest reason why my daughter should be dragged into this unpleasant business. Inspector: (impressively) I'm a police inspector, miss Birling. This afternoon a young woman drank some disinfectant, and died, after several hours of agony, tonight in the infirmary." – When Sheila enters and the Inspector insists she stay, Birling attacks his authority, only to be met with a stark restatement of Eva Smith's death (Act 1)
quote_zh: Birling's choice of 'officious' is a weapon of class power. By branding the Inspector's professional conduct as meddlesome, he attempts to diminish a working man's right to question his social superiors. The word carries connotations of petty bureaucracy, a sneer from a capitalist who believes his status as former Lord Mayor should shield his family. The Inspector, unruffled, replies not by defending his manner but by reasserting the brutal facts of Eva's suicide. Priestley's juxtaposition exposes the hollowness of Birling's outrage: while Birling frets about being inconvenienced, a woman has died in agony. The adjective 'officious' thus marks the precise point where self-interest collides with social duty.
ex_en: Birling's complaint that the inquiry was officious revealed his deeper belief that the wealthy should be exempt from the scrutiny that defends the powerless.
ex_zh: The word 'officious' fell from Birling's mouth like a heavy door shutting out a beggar, its polished brass plate reading 'Not at Home to Conscience'.

def_en: gloomy, drab, and dirty-looking from neglect
def_zh: dingy room, dingy little, dingy back bedroom
quote_en: "Inspector: I've thought that it would do us all a bit of good if sometimes we tried to put ourselves in the place of these young women counting their pennies, in their dingy little back bedrooms. Sheila: Yes, I expect it would. But what happened to her then?" – After describing Eva Smith's unemployment and loneliness, the Inspector reproaches the family's lack of imagination; Sheila, beginning to feel sympathy, asks what came next (Act 1)
quote_zh: 'Dingy' paints a miniature picture of poverty in a single adjective. The word evokes dirt, insufficient light, and neglect—the physical expression of a society that discards its young women. Priestley puts this sensory detail into the Inspector's mouth deliberately, contrasting the 'dingy little back bedrooms' with the Birlings' own dining room, described in the opening stage directions as having 'good solid furniture' and a 'heavily comfortable' atmosphere. Where the Birlings enjoy pink, intimate lighting, the unseen Eva counts pennies in a space the adjective 'dingy' renders almost claustrophobic. Sheila's immediate assent—"I expect it would"—is a crucial step in her moral awakening; she is learning to see what her comfort has hidden from view.
ex_en: The Inspector forced Sheila to imagine the dingy back bedroom where Eva Smith had counted her pennies, and that act of imagination began to dismantle her self-satisfaction.
ex_zh: The guilt crept into Sheila's mind like the damp that spreads through a dingy room, invisible at first but staining everything it touched.

def_en: extremely angry
def_zh: absolutely furious, furious temper, furious with
quote_en: "Inspector: And why did you do that? Sheila: Because I was in a furious temper. Inspector: And what had this girl done to make you lose your temper. Sheila: When I was looking at myself in the mirror I caught sight of her smiling at the assistant, and I was furious with her." – Sheila confesses to the Inspector how a moment of jealousy and wounded vanity led her to force Eva Smith's dismissal from Milwards (Act 1)
quote_zh: The repetition of 'furious' in Sheila's confession exposes the dangerous gulf between emotion and consequence. Priestley does not allow her to soften the word: she was not merely annoyed or cross, but 'absolutely furious', a phrase that carries the ungovernable heat of a tantrum. The mirror, a symbol of her own vanity, becomes the trigger: she sees the assistant smiling and, in that reflection, reads a judgment on her own inadequacy. The adjective thus connects personal insecurity to the exercise of cruel social power. By forcing the word out of her, the Inspector shows that trivial emotions, when armed with money and status, can destroy a life. Sheila's willingness to own the word 'furious' marks the beginning of her moral honesty, and the audience begins to distinguish her from her unrepentant father.
ex_en: Sheila's furious outburst in the shop, though it lasted only minutes, set off a chain of events that no apology could undo because a life had already been extinguished.
ex_zh: Fury, like a struck match, flared in Sheila's chest—a brief, searing light that consumed her common sense and left only ash in its wake.

def_en: not showing proper respect; rude and presumptuous
def_zh: impertinent remark, trifle impertinent, grossly impertinent
quote_en: "Inspector: I said yes - I do understand her. And she's right. Mrs Birling: that - I consider - is a trifle impertinent, inspector. // Sheila gives a short hysterical laugh //" – After the Inspector supports Sheila's insistence on hearing the whole truth, Mrs Birling uses the language of social offence to put him in his place (Act 2)
quote_zh: Mrs Birling's weapon is the adverb 'impertinent'. The word belongs to the vocabulary of class hierarchy: it defines an offence not against morality but against deference. By calling the Inspector's plain agreement with her daughter 'a trifle impertinent', Mrs Birling attempts to reduce a crisis of conscience to a breach of manners. Priestley underlines the absurdity of this deflection with Sheila's hysterical laugh—a sound that cuts through her mother's drawing-room pretensions. The adjective 'impertinent' thus becomes a marker of the older generation's moral blindness: they can only perceive disrespect, never their own culpability. The Inspector, famously unoffendable, simply absorbs the insult, and the audience understands that it is Mrs Birling who is on trial.
ex_en: Mrs Birling's charge of impertinence revealed that she equated morality with social rank, so that any challenge to her authority was automatically a discourtesy rather than a call to conscience.
ex_zh: 'Impertinent', she murmured, drawing the word around her like a silk shawl against a cold wind of truth.

def_en: arrogantly superior and disdainful
def_zh: haughty manner, haughty tone, haughty refusal
quote_en: "Inspector: I said yes - I do understand her. And she's right. Mrs Birling: (haughtily) I beg your pardon!" – Mrs Birling reacts to the Inspector's affirmation of Sheila's wish to remain, her tone freezing the room with injured dignity (Act 2)
quote_zh: The italicized stage direction 'haughtily' in this exchange is as devastating as any spoken line. Mrs Birling's words are few—merely a formal demand for the Inspector to retract—but the manner Priestley prescribes transforms them into a weapon of class condescension. 'Haughtily' suggests an elevated chin, a cold stare; it conveys that she believes herself so far above the Inspector that even his existence is an impertinence. This one adverb encapsulates her entire moral stance throughout the scene: she will later speak of 'girls of that class' with the same unthinking superiority. Priestley uses the word to expose how social haughtiness can harden into cruelty when faced with a plea for help. The Inspector's refusal to bow to it is his quiet victory.
ex_en: Mrs Birling's haughty refusal to be questioned was the same arrogance that had closed the committee's door on a desperate, pregnant girl.
ex_zh: Haughtiness sat upon her features like a permanent frost, preserving her face in an elegance that would shatter at the lightest touch of human warmth.

def_en: feeling or appearing troubled or nervous; physically disturbed
def_zh: increasingly agitated, visibly agitated, agitated voice
quote_en: "Birling: I must say, Sybil, that when this comes out at the inquest, it isn't going to do us much good. The press might easily take it up- - Mrs Birling: (agitated now) Oh, stop it, both of you. And please remember before you start accusing me of anything again that it wasn't I who had her turned out of her employment - - which probably began it all." – As the Inspector's questioning intensifies, Birling worries about public scandal, and Mrs Birling, her composure cracking, tries to deflect blame onto her husband and daughter (Act 2)
quote_zh: The stage direction 'agitated now' signals the first fissure in Mrs Birling's marble composure. Until this moment she has met the Inspector with cool disdain, but the mention of the inquest and the press punctures her self-assurance. 'Agitated' suggests not simply anxiety but a loss of physical control—a trembling of the hands, a catch in the voice—that betrays the panic beneath her principles. Priestley chooses this adjective to mark the point at which public exposure becomes more terrifying to Mrs Birling than private guilt. In her desperation she reaches for the oldest weapon: accusation. Yet her claim that 'it wasn't I who had her turned out' rings hollow precisely because the audience has seen her own cold refusal of aid. 'Agitated' is the herald of her moral collapse; it reveals that her righteousness was all posture.
ex_en: Mrs Birling became agitated not because she recognised her cruelty, but because she realised it might cost her the public respectability she valued above all else.
ex_zh: An agitated whispering crept into her voice, like the first stirrings of a loose shutter before a storm that was about to tear the whole house apart.

def_en: a person who is responsible for a crime or other misdeed
def_zh: chief culprit, find the culprit, punish the culprit
quote_en: "Inspector: so he's the chief culprit anyhow. Mrs Birling: certainly. And he ought to be dealt with very severely - - Sheila: (with sudden alarm) mother - stop - stop!" – Mrs Birling, unaware that the unknown father of Eva's child is her own son Eric, demands the harshest punishment for him; Sheila, understanding the truth, tries to silence her (Act 2)
quote_zh: 'Culprit' is the pivot upon which the entire scene of dramatic irony turns. Mrs Birling speaks the word with the full weight of magisterial certainty, imagining a faceless 'drunken young idler' who deserves no mercy. Priestley gives her the language of the courtroom—'dealt with very severely'—and then freezes the audience in horror as they realise she is condemning her own son. The word 'culprit' thus becomes a trap: it locks Mrs Birling into a position from which she cannot escape when the truth is revealed. Sheila's desperate 'stop - stop!' is the voice of knowledge trying to halt the avalanche, but it is too late. Through this single noun, Priestley demonstrates that the line between judge and guilty party is thinner than the proud imagine.
ex_en: In demanding that the culprit face public disgrace, Mrs Birling unknowingly wrote her own family's indictment and sealed her son's silent agony.
ex_zh: The word 'culprit', once spoken, lay in the middle of the room like a loaded revolver that everyone but Mrs Birling could see was pointed at her own heart.

def_en: so shocked or surprised as to be unable to speak
def_zh: stare dumbfounded, left dumbfounded, utterly dumbfounded
quote_en: "Birling: That was the police. A girl has just died - on her way to the Infirmary - after swallowing some disinfectant. And a police inspector is on his way here - to ask some - questions - - - // As they stare guiltily and dumbfounded, the curtain falls.//" – After the Birlings and Gerald have convinced themselves the Inspector was a hoax, the telephone rings with news that an actual suicide has occurred and a real inspector is coming; the play ends on their frozen shock (Act 3)
quote_zh: The final stage direction 'dumbfounded' is Priestley's masterstroke. After the nervous laughter of relief, the word shatters all complacency and leaves the family—and the audience—in a suspended moment of horror. 'Dumbfounded' denotes a shock so profound that speech is impossible, and in that silence the moral weight of the entire play descends. The adverb 'guiltily' preceding it reminds us that even if the Inspector was a hoax, their confessions were real. Priestley refuses a comfortable resolution: the family is not absolved, merely given a second chance, and whether they will learn remains an open question. 'Dumbfounded' is the sound of an unlearnt lesson echoing in a room that, moments before, had been filled with self-congratulation.
ex_en: The final tableau of the Birlings, dumbfounded by the phone call, left the audience with the uneasy question of whether any of them—except perhaps Sheila and Eric—had truly understood the Inspector's message.
ex_zh: Dumbfounded silence rushed in like a black tide, drowning their laughter and leaving only the white faces of people who had just heard their own epitaph read aloud.

def_en: to twist or weave together so as to be inseparably connected
def_zh: closely intertwined, intricately intertwined, lives intertwined
quote_en: "Inspector: ... their lives, their hopes and fears, their suffering and chance of happiness, all intertwined with our lives, and what we think and say and do. We don't live alone. We are members of one body. We are responsible for each other." – The Inspector's parting speech, in which he articulates the moral vision of collective responsibility that each family member has failed to honour (Act 3)
quote_zh: 'Intertwined' is the thematic keystone of Priestley's socialist message. By choosing this organic, almost physical verb, he insists that human lives are not separate threads but a single fabric. The word rejects Birling's earlier creed—'a man has to look after himself'—by asserting that the poverty of Eva Smith is woven directly into the prosperity of the Birlings. The image of weaving suggests both complexity and inescapable connection; one cannot pull a single thread without unravelling the whole. Priestley places this word at the climax of the play, after all the confessions have been made, so that the audience leaves with a single, luminous idea: that responsibility is not a burden to be avoided but the very texture of being human. The Inspector's use of 'intertwined' transforms his speech from a police inquiry into a sermon.
ex_en: The Inspector's final word 'intertwined' dismantled Mr Birling's entire philosophy, replacing self-interest with a vision of society as a single fabric in which every thread matters.
ex_zh: The fates of the Birlings and Eva Smith were intertwined like roots beneath a forest floor, invisible to those who walked above but feeding the same dark, necessary truth.

def_en: a sweet, fortified red wine typically drunk after a meal
def_zh: a glass of port, decanter of port, judge of port
quote_en: "Arthur Birling: Giving us the port, Edna? That's right. (he pushes it towards Eric..) you ought to like this port, Gerald, as a matter of fact, Finchley told me it's exactly the same port your father gets from him. Gerald: Then it'll be all right. The governor prides himself on being a good judge of port. I don't pretend to know much about it. Sheila: (gaily, possessively) I should jolly well think not, Gerald, I'd hate you to know all about port - like one of these purple- faced old men." – The family, having finished dessert, accept the decanter of port from the maid Edna while Sheila teasingly warns Gerald against becoming a stuffy connoisseur (Act 1)
quote_zh: The word 'port' operates as a badge of middle-class masculine identity in 1912 Brumley. Birling's offering—and his boast that it matches the Crofts' supply—is a transparent social manoeuvre, attempting to flaunt his own refinement while latching onto Gerald's higher status. The entire moment is steeped in ritual: the men linger over port while the women are expected to withdraw. Priestley uses this custom to expose the gender and class assumptions of the period. Sheila's mock-horror at the thought of 'a purple- faced old man' punctures the complacency, but her light remark also touches on the genuine moral ugliness beneath the pleasantries. The port, rich and celebratory, will soon become merely an emblem of the wealth and security that Eva Smith could never afford—and that the Birling family misuses.
ex_en: The decanter of port on the dining table, smooth as the family's evening ritual, held the bitter aftertaste of a life they had destroyed without ever tasting its contents.
ex_zh: The port glowed in the glass like liquid ambergris, its sweetness a perfect mask for the rot creeping through the solid mahogany of the Birling furniture.

def_en: expressing disapproval, blame, or disappointment
def_zh: reproachful glance, reproachful tone, reproachful manner
quote_en: "Birling: Well, well - this is very nice. Very nice. Good dinner too, Sybil. Tell cook from me. Gerald: (politely) Absolutely first class. Mrs Birling: (reproachfully) Arthur, you're not supposed to say such things." – Immediately after Edna leaves, Birling compliments the dinner in an overly effusive way; Gerald echoes the praise, and Mrs Birling scolds her husband for speaking too openly in front of a guest (Act 1)
quote_zh: The stage direction 'reproachfully' introduces the audience to the cool, controlling vein that runs beneath Mrs Birling's gracious exterior. Her reproach is not for the content of what Arthur says—after all, she herself will soon demand propriety from her children—but for its lack of social polish. Birling has, in her eyes, lowered himself by speaking directly and warmly about the food, a breach of the code that governs dinner parties. Priestley uses this tiny flash of marital tension to demonstrate that the Birlings' manners are a performance, and even the smallest slip invites correction. The adverb also foreshadows the far graver reproaches to come: when the Inspector enters, Mrs Birling will be the last to accept any moral blame, clinging to a sense of her own rectitude until her son's confession destroys it.
ex_en: Mrs Birling's reproachful glance at her husband was the first sign that her world was governed by the fear of appearing common, a fear that would later prove more powerful than compassion.
ex_zh: The reproachful silence that followed her words was heavier than any shout, a velvet-covered hammer that drove the nail of correctness through every spontaneous joy.

def_en: of or concerning the regions outside the capital city, especially when regarded as unsophisticated or narrow-minded
def_zh: provincial speech, provincial attitudes, rather provincial
quote_en: "Arthur Birling is a heavy- looking, rather portentous man in this middle fifties with fairly easy manners but rather provincial in this speech." – The opening stage directions introduce the family patriarch through a detailed physical and social description, setting him up as a man whose aspirations exceed his origins (Act 1)
quote_zh: Priestley's use of 'provincial' in the stage directions is a devastating piece of character assassination delivered before Birling has spoken a single line. The adjective suggests an accent, a vocabulary, and a set of reference points that are not those of the metropolitan elite. Combined with 'rather portentous', it sketches a man who is self-important without possessing genuine authority, a man of business who will never entirely escape the taint of the factory. Birling's later boasts—about his knighthood, his friendship with the Chief Constable, his sound judgments—are all attempts to transcend this provincial label. The word becomes the engine of his insecurity, driving him to prove himself a gentleman and thus blinding him to the moral demands that a true public figure might heed.
ex_en: Birling's provincial speech betrayed the very social inferiority he spent his life trying to bury under port, silver, and knighthoods.
ex_zh: The provincial accent clung to his words like the scent of machine oil to the hands of a worker who has scrubbed himself into a stiff collar and hoped no one would notice.

def_en: a man unduly concerned with a stylish and fashionable appearance
def_zh: too manly to be a dandy, dress like a dandy, young dandy
quote_en: "Gerald Croft is an attractive chap about thirty, rather too manly to be a dandy but very much the well- bred young man- about- town." – The stage directions describe Gerald Croft as the couple prepares to dine, placing him precisely in the social hierarchy through his bearing and dress (Act 1)
quote_zh: Priestley's contrast between 'dandy' and 'manly' positions Gerald as the ideal Edwardian gentleman: elegant but not effeminate, fashionable but not frivolous. The word 'dandy' carries historical weight, evoking the foppish Regency aristocrats who valued appearance above substance. By assuring us that Gerald is 'rather too manly' for such a label, the stage directions subtly endorse the commercial, practical masculinity that Birling also admires. Yet this carefully constructed image will unravel as the play proceeds: Gerald's affair with Daisy Renton reveals a weakness beneath the polish, and his eventual exposure shows that the line between a gentleman and a seducer is perilously thin. The term 'dandy' thus sets up the idea that outward charm can mask moral emptiness.
ex_en: Gerald's near-dandyish polish made it all the more shocking to discover that, underneath the expensive tailoring, he was just another young man who used a vulnerable woman for his own comfort.
ex_zh: He dressed like a dandy and spoke like a diplomat, but when the light fell at a certain angle, the audience could see that his kindness was pressed as flat and temporary as the crease in his trousers.

def_en: having a confident and forceful manner, often bordering on aggression
def_zh: assertive manner, half assertive, overly assertive
quote_en: "Eric is in his early twenties, not quite at ease, half shy, half assertive." – The stage directions introduce Eric Birling as a young man oscillating between timidity and uncertain aggression, already hinting at the internal tension that will erupt later (Act 1)
quote_zh: The phrase 'half shy, half assertive' captures the adolescent paralysis that defines Eric throughout the play. Priestley does not allow his audience to settle on a fixed impression; instead, the adverb 'half' suggests a self divided against itself. Eric's assertiveness is not confidence but compensation—an attempt to claim the adult authority his father wields so effortlessly. When he later bursts out with "Well, I think it's a dam' shame" or guffaws unexpectedly, these are the spasms of an unformed man trying to find his voice. The word 'assertive' thus becomes a diagnosis of impotence masked as strength, a quality that will prove disastrous when he encounters Eva Smith and, in his drunken state, forces himself upon her.
ex_en: Eric's half-assertive manner was a clue to the guilt that would later consume him: he wanted to be decisive like his father but lacked the emotional vocabulary to do anything except destroy.
ex_zh: The assertive crack in his voice was like a fist clenched inside a velvet glove—the punch never landed, but the trembling of the fabric revealed everything.

def_en: causing general public outrage by a perceived offence against morality or propriety
def_zh: scandalous affair, scandalous behaviour, nothing scandalous
quote_en: "Birling: Look - there's nothing mysterious - or scandalous - about this business - at least not so far as I'm concerned. It's a perfectly straightforward case, and as it happened more than eighteen months ago - nearly two years ago - obviously it has nothing whatever to do with the wretched girl's suicide. Eh, inspector? Inspector: No, sir. I can't agree with you there. Birling: Why not? Inspector: Because what happened to her then may have determined what happened to her afterwards, and what happened to her afterwards may have driven her to suicide. A chain of events." – Birling attempts to dismiss the Inspector's inquiry by asserting that his own actions could not possibly be scandalous or relevant to the suicide (Act 1)
quote_zh: Birling reaches for the word 'scandalous' as a shield. To him, the opposite of scandalous is not 'moral' but 'ordinary', and he believes that a routine business decision—sacking a troublemaker—carries no shame. Priestley's Inspector demolishes this logic with the simple counter-image of a 'chain of events', a metaphor that redefines the moral universe so that no act is isolated. The adjective 'scandalous', so often associated with sexual disgrace, is here applied to the whole fabric of capitalist indifference. Birling cannot see that his own refusal to pay a living wage might be the greatest scandal of all. The word thus marks the play's central inversion: what society calls respectable is often monstrous, and what it calls scandal may simply be the truth dragged into light.
ex_en: Birling's insistence that his actions were not scandalous revealed that his moral compass was calibrated not by justice but by public reputation.
ex_zh: The scandal of Eva's death was not a thunderclap but a slow, soundless rot that spread from the factory floor to the infirmary bed, unnoticed by anyone who mattered.

def_en: in a very unhappy or unfortunate state; of poor quality
def_zh: wretched girl, wretched business, wretched creature
quote_en: "Birling: It's a perfectly straightforward case, and as it happened more than eighteen months ago - nearly two years ago - obviously it has nothing whatever to do with the wretched girl's suicide. Eh, inspector?" – Birling, still trying to dismiss the Inspector, refers to Eva Smith as 'the wretched girl' in a tone that mixes pity with impatience (Act 1)
quote_zh: The adjective 'wretched' slips from Birling's mouth almost automatically, revealing his deepest attitudes. To him, Eva Smith is not an individual with a name and a story; she is a type—a 'case', a 'business', and finally a 'wretched girl' whose misfortunes cannot possibly be his concern. The word drips condescension: it allows Birling to express a shallow, performative pity while simultaneously dismissing the girl as insignificant. Priestley exposes the cruelty latent in such language by planting this adjective just before the Inspector delivers his devastating 'chain of events' speech. The audience sees that Birling's 'wretched' is a label slapped onto anyone poor enough to be an inconvenience, a word that absolves the speaker of responsibility by reducing a human life to a pitiable abstraction.
ex_en: Birling called her the 'wretched girl' because if he had called her 'Eva Smith'—a person with a face and a past—he would have had to admit that his hands were stained.
ex_zh: The wretched truth knelt at the edge of the dining table like a beggar refused admission, her fingers leaving invisible smears on the polished silver.

def_en: a worker who supervises and directs other workers
def_zh: works foreman, shop foreman, foreman told me
quote_en: "Birling: She was a lively good- looking girl - country- bred, I fancy - and she'd been working in one of our machine shops for over a year. A good worker too. In fact, the foreman there told me he was ready to promote her into what we call a leading operator - head of a small group of girls." – Birling recalls the details of Eva Smith's employment after the Inspector shows him the photograph, speaking of her as a promising employee (Act 1)
quote_zh: The word 'foreman' in Birling's narrative is the human face of a system he otherwise discusses in abstractions—'labour costs', 'rates', 'percentages'. The foreman, a practical man who sees the day-to-day value of a worker, judged Eva worthy of promotion. Priestley uses this detail to sharpen the indictment: it was not incompetence that led to her dismissal but a strike for a marginally higher wage. The foreman's approval of Eva stands as silent testimony that she was the exact opposite of a troublemaker—a loyal, capable worker. That Birling can recount this and still defend her sacking reveals a mind so compartmentalised that human worth and economic logic have become entirely separate. The foreman's voice, unheard except through Birling's recollection, becomes the ghost of a fairer judgment that was overruled by profit.
ex_en: The foreman's praise of Eva Smith, casually mentioned by Birling, hung in the air like an accusation that would not settle until the Inspector gave it a name.
ex_zh: The foreman's thumbs-up had faded from Eva's memory like chalk off a factory wall, leaving only the faintest trace of a world where hard work was still supposed to count for something.

def_en: evoking or deserving pity; pathetically small or inadequate
def_zh: pitiful affair, pitiful excuse, pitiful attempt
quote_en: "Birling: So they went on strike. That didn't last long, of course. Gerald: Not if it was just after the holidays. They'd be all broke - if I know them. Birling: Right, Gerald. They mostly were. And so was the strike, after a week or two. Pitiful affair." – Birling recounts with contempt the failed strike that Eva Smith helped lead, dismissing the workers' collective action as feeble and doomed (Act 1)
quote_zh: Birling's 'pitiful affair' is a three-word victory speech. The adjective is chosen to mock not only the strike's failure but the very idea that workers might demand better treatment. By branding the struggle 'pitiful', Birling reduces the desperate hopes of his employees to a shabby, almost comical spectacle. Priestley ensures that the audience hears the class condescension in every syllable: the strike was pitiful because the workers were hungry and broke—facts that make their resistance more heroic, not less, had Birling any moral imagination. The word also achieves a terrible irony when set against the play's conclusion, where Eva's whole life might be described as a 'pitiful affair' of poverty and despair, and yet no one responsible for it will shed a tear.
ex_en: The strike that Birling dismissed as a pitiful affair was, for Eva Smith, the last time she stood with her fellow workers before the long, lonely slide into the infirmary.
ex_zh: Pitiful as a child's fist against a locked factory gate, the strike rattled for a week and then fell silent, its echo only returning years later in the shape of a policeman's knock.

def_en: dismissed from employment
def_zh: get sacked, being sacked, sacked from
quote_en: "Eric: Well, I think it's a dam' shame. Inspector: No, I've never wanted to play. Eric: No, I mean about this girl - Eva Smith. Why shouldn't they try for higher wages? We try for the highest possible prices. And I don't see why she should have been sacked just because she'd a bit more spirit than the others. You said yourself she was a good worker. I'd have let her stay." – After hearing his father's account of the dismissal, Eric bursts out in defence of Eva, using the blunt word that his parents have been avoiding (Act 1)
quote_zh: Eric's use of 'sacked' cuts through the evasions that have preceded it. Where Birling speaks of 'discharging', 'letting go', or 'clearing out', Eric seizes on the blunt, colloquial term—the word the workers themselves would use. It sounds crude at the dining table, and that is precisely its power. The verb carries the violence of a sudden blow, the experience of being thrown out of a community without appeal. By coupling it with the admission that Eva had 'spirit', Eric aligns himself, however clumsily, with the Inspector's moral perspective. The word becomes a small rebellion against his father's euphemistic capitalism, a flash of the decency that will later be buried under the revelation of his own far graver betrayal.
ex_en: When Eric finally said 'sacked' aloud, the dinner table fell silent, as if the word had torn a hole in the dam of careful language through which all the unspoken truths began to pour.
ex_zh: She was sacked like a stone dropped from a moving train: one moment part of the machine's rhythm, the next a speck on the tracks that no one bothered to look back at.

def_en: completely confused and uncertain about what to think or do
def_zh: exchange bewildered glances, look bewildered, bewildered expression
quote_en: "Birling: (with marked change of tone) Well, of course, if I'd known that earlier, I wouldn't have called you officious and talked about reporting you. ... You sure of your facts? Inspector: Some of them - yes. Birling: I can't think they can be of any great consequence. Inspector: The girl's dead though. Sheila: What do you mean by saying that? You talk as if we were responsible- - Birling: (cutting in) Just a minute, Sheila. Now, inspector, perhaps you and I had better go and talk this over quietly in a corner- - Sheila: (cutting in) Why should you? He's finished with you. He says it's one of us now. // the other four exchange bewildered and perturbed glances.//" – After the Inspector reveals that he has not come solely to question Birling, the family and Gerald are thrown into confusion, their earlier certainty dissolving (Act 1)
quote_zh: Priestley's stage direction 'bewildered' transforms the Birlings' dining room into a place of sudden, shared disorientation. The word denotes a loss of mental compass, a state in which familiar hierarchies and assumptions no longer provide reliable guidance. Up to this point, Birling has controlled the conversation; now, in a single line—"He says it's one of us now"—Sheila dismantles the protective barrier between the public realm of business and the private conscience. The pairing of 'bewildered' with 'perturbed' adds a tremor of anxiety, suggesting that confusion is about to give way to fear. Priestley uses this collective bewilderment to foreshadow the individual confessions that will follow, as each character is forced to confront a version of events they cannot immediately control.
ex_en: The bewildered silence that followed the Inspector's implication was the moment the family's united front cracked, letting in the first cold draught of individual accountability.
ex_zh: Bewilderment spread through the room like ink dropped into water, swirling away every clear outline until all that remained was a shapeless, deepening dread.

def_en: affected by or deriving from violently uncontrolled emotion; wildly irrational
def_zh: hysterical laugh, hysterical outburst, become hysterical
quote_en: "Gerald: We can keep it from him. Sheila: (laughs rather hysterically) why - you fool - he knows. Of course he knows. And I hate to think how much he knows that we don't know yet. You'll see. You'll see. // she looks at him almost in triumph. He looks crushed.//" – After the Inspector reveals Eva Smith's alias 'Daisy Renton' and Gerald's guilt becomes obvious, Gerald begs Sheila to hide the truth; she, already bitterly enlightened, laughs at his naivety (Act 1)
quote_zh: The stage direction 'hysterically' marks a threshold in Sheila's character development. Her laughter is not amusement but the release of unbearable tension—a gift of insight that has arrived too late to save anyone. Priestley uses the adjective to show that the moral awakening the Inspector brings is not gentle but jolting; it shatters the comfortable narratives that the characters have lived by. This hysterical edge also embodies the play's gothic undertone: the knowledge Sheila now possesses is so terrible that it verges on madness. In the context of 1912 gender expectations, a young woman's hysteria might have been dismissed as feminine weakness; Priestley reclaims it as a form of terrible lucidity, the only sane response to an insane moral blindness.
ex_en: Sheila's hysterical laughter, directed at Gerald's pathetic attempt at concealment, was the sound of a comfortable illusion being torn apart, never to be stitched back together.
ex_zh: The truth came out of Sheila in a hysterical burst, like a scream that had been sealed in a champagne bottle and shaken for two years.

def_en: very unpleasant, horrifying, or disagreeable
def_zh: horrid business, horrid affair, horrid death
quote_en: "Inspector: Yes, she was in great agony. They did everything they could for her at the infirmary, but she died. Suicide, of course. Birling: (rather impatiently) Yes, yes. Horrid business. But I don't understand why you should come here, inspector -" – The Inspector has just described Eva Smith's death in graphic terms, and Birling attempts to brush it aside with a single, dismissive adjective (Act 1)
quote_zh: Birling's 'horrid business' is a linguistic shrug. The word 'horrid' is childlike, almost squeamish—a term one might apply to a rainy day or a disappointing pudding—and utterly inadequate to the 'agony' the Inspector has just described. Priestley exposes Birling's moral smallness through this lexical mismatch: while the Inspector offers the granular horror of 'burnt her inside out', Birling can only muster a prim distaste. The phrase 'Horrid business' reduces a human being's final despair to an inconvenience, something to be hastily acknowledged and then set aside. It is the perfect verbal embodiment of the capitalist callousness that the play indicts, a politeness that can register suffering only as a social embarrassment.
ex_en: To dismiss a girl's suicide as a 'horrid business' was to treat her death as one might a broken engagement or a failed investment—a minor unpleasantness to be buried as quickly as possible.
ex_zh: The word 'horrid' fell from Birling's lips like a single cold drop of tea on a white tablecloth, barely staining the surface while the body was still being scrubbed off the infirmary floor.

def_en: in a smug or self-satisfied manner, without awareness of real danger
def_zh: smile complacently, laugh complacently, remark complacently
quote_en: "Birling: You see, I was lord mayor here two years ago when royalty visited us. And I've always been regarded as a sound useful party man. So - well - I gather there's a very good chance of a knighthood - so long as we behave ourselves, don't get into the police court or start a scandal - eh? (laughs complacently.)" – Alone with Gerald, Birling confides his social ambitions, taking for granted that his family's respectability is unassailable (Act 1)
quote_zh: Priestley's choice of the adverb 'complacently' is a time-bomb planted in Act 1 that detonates throughout the rest of the play. The word shares its root with 'complacent', derived from Latin *complacere* (to please greatly), and it captures precisely Birling's fatal self-satisfaction. His laughter is not the easy chuckle of a confident man but the smugness of one who believes he has already won. The dramatic irony is scalding: the audience knows that the very scandal Birling jokes about is already waiting at the door. Priestley uses the adverb to warn that complacency—the unquestioning assumption that one's privileges are both deserved and permanent—is not a harmless social grace but a moral blindfold.
ex_en: Birling's complacently stated condition for his knighthood—'so long as we behave ourselves'—became, in hindsight, the darkest joke in the play, for every member of his family had already misbehaved in ways that would destroy an ordinary man.
ex_zh: Complacency lay over the dinner table like thick icing on a rotten cake, its smooth sweetness hiding the decay that only needed a knife's pressure to reveal.

def_en: serious and unrelenting in manner, especially in the enforcement of discipline or moral authority
def_zh: stern look, stern voice, sternly warn
quote_en: "Mrs Birling: ...she was giving herself ridiculous airs. She was claiming elaborate fine feelings and scruples that were simply absurd in a girl in her position. Inspector: (very sternly) Her position now is that she lies with a burnt- out inside on a ... (page missing) ... I'm losing all patience with you people. What did she say?" – After Mrs Birling dismisses Eva Smith's moral scruples as absurd and pretentious, the Inspector's patience finally cracks; his sternness is no longer professional neutrality but righteous fury (Act 2)
quote_zh: The stage direction 'very sternly' signals the Inspector's transition from clinical interrogator to moral accuser. 'Stern' derives from Old English *styrne*, meaning severe or strict, and it carries the authority of a parent or judge who has exhausted all tolerance. Priestley deploys this word at the precise moment when Mrs Birling's class contempt has become unbearable even to the audience. The Inspector's sternness is not cruelty but the last resource of a man who has tried reason and now must wield force. Crucially, his sternness is directed not at Eva's actions but at the language used to dismiss her—'ridiculous airs', 'absurd'—revealing that the real obscenity in the room is the cold-blooded superiority of the respectable woman.
ex_en: The Inspector's stern rebuke cut through Mrs Birling's self-regard like a surgeon's scalpel, exposing the dead tissue of her compassion.
ex_zh: His voice, stern as an iron bell struck in an empty church, tolled not for Eva Smith alone but for every soul who had mistaken comfort for virtue.

def_en: famous or well known, typically for some bad quality or deed
def_zh: notorious womanizer, notorious for, notorious reputation
quote_en: "Gerald: ...Old Joe Meggarty, half- drunk and goggle- eyed, had wedged her into a corner with that obscene fat carcass of his- - Mrs Birling: (cutting in) there's no need to be disgusting. And surely you don't mean Alderman Meggarty? Gerald: of course I do. He's a notorious womanizer as well as being one of the worst sots and rogues in Brumley- - Inspector: Quite right." – Gerald describes the circumstances in which he first met the desperate Daisy Renton, and Mrs Birling's shock at the name of a public figure confirms the double standard that protects respectable abusers (Act 2)
quote_zh: 'Notorious' is the weight that collapses the false distinction between public virtue and private vice. By applying the word to Alderman Meggarty—a fellow magistrate and pillar of the community—Gerald forces the Birlings to acknowledge that the very social order they worship is rotten with sexual exploitation. Mrs Birling's instinctive 'Surely you don't mean...' betrays that she knows exactly what Meggarty is but has chosen, as a matter of social discretion, to un-know it. The Inspector's flat confirmation—'Quite right'—aligns the play's moral authority with the unvarnished truth. Priestley uses the adjective 'notorious' to strip away the mask that men like Birling wear to the golf club, revealing that respectability and predation can coexist in the same body.
ex_en: The notorious Alderman Meggarty, a 'sound useful party man' in public and a sexual predator in private, was the living proof that Birling's world of honours and gentleman's agreements was built on a foundation of unpunished cruelty.
ex_zh: Notoriety clung to Meggarty like the stench of stale gin in a velvet waistcoat, a secret everyone knew and no one mentioned until the Inspector forced their mouths open.

def_en: a loud and hearty burst of laughter, often coarse or unrestrained
def_zh: let out a guffaw, sudden guffaw, guffaw loudly
quote_en: "Eric suddenly guffaws. His parents look at him. Sheila: (severely) Now - what's the joke? Eric: I don't know - really. Suddenly I felt I just had to laugh." – During the engagement dinner, in the middle of an affectionate exchange between Sheila and Gerald, Eric bursts out with an uncontrollable, inexplicable laugh (Act 1)
quote_zh: Eric's 'guffaw' is the first discordant note in the Birlings' orchestrated evening. The noun suggests something loud, involuntary, and slightly indecent—a sound that belongs to a bar-room, not a dining room with champagne and port. Priestley uses this unexpected noise to signal that Eric is already out of step with the family performance; his body is betraying the tension that his words cannot express. His parents' stare of disapproval and Sheila's severe 'What's the joke?' are miniature rehearsals of the judgment that will later descend on him. The guffaw, moreover, is a pre-emptive echo of the drunken laughter that will colour his confession of how he raped Eva Smith. It is a physical outbreak of the chaos that the Birlings' politeness can only temporarily contain.
ex_en: Eric's sudden guffaw, incomprehensible to himself, was the first sign that the careful, self-congratulatory atmosphere of the dinner could be shattered by a single, unruly impulse from the dark places the family refused to acknowledge.
ex_zh: The guffaw exploded from Eric like a cork from a bottle of spoiled champagne, spraying an ugly froth over the immaculate tablecloth before anyone could catch it.

def_en: a humorous or malicious deception; a practical joke or elaborate trick
def_zh: perpetrate a hoax, elaborate hoax, cruel hoax
quote_en: "Gerald: What do you make of this business now? Was it a hoax? Birling: Of course. Somebody put that fellow up to coming here and hoaxing us. There are people in this town who dislike me enough to do that. We ought to have seen through it from the first." – After discovering that no Inspector Goole exists on the police force, Birling seizes on the idea of a hoax to wrap up the evening's unpleasantness and restore his sense of control (Act 3)
quote_zh: The word 'hoax' is Birling's escape route, and Priestley allows him to run down it at full speed precisely to demonstrate its inadequacy. A 'hoax' implies a trick with a punchline, and Birling's insistence that the whole affair was a piece of targeted mischief against him personally reveals his incorrigible self-centredness. Even after confessing his own actions, he can only understand events through the lens of his reputation. The irony is scalding: Birling wants to treat as a joke what was, in fact, a meticulously accurate moral dissection. The word also serves Priestley's broader dramatic purpose—by raising and then appearing to dismiss the hoax possibility before the final phone call, he ensures that the audience feels the ground vanish twice.
ex_en: Birling's eagerness to brand the Inspector's visit a hoax exposed his fundamental refusal to learn: he would rather believe himself the victim of a cruel joke than accept that his actions had contributed to a real girl's death.
ex_zh: The hoax theory comforted the Birlings like a glass of warm milk, settling their nerves just in time for the telephone to shatter the glass against the wall.

def_en: a person who advocates socialism, a political and economic theory of social organisation based on collective or governmental ownership
def_zh: socialist or crank, avowed socialist, socialist sympathies
quote_en: "Birling: That fellow obviously didn't like us. He was prejudiced from the start. Probably a socialist or some sort of crank - he talked like one. And then, instead of standing up to him, you let him bluff you into talking about your private affairs." – In the aftermath of the Inspector's departure, as the family attempts to reconstruct its shattered confidence, Birling dismisses the Inspector's moral authority by reducing it to a political label (Act 3)
quote_zh: Birling's use of 'socialist' as a term of dismissal is the most politically explicit moment in the play, and Priestley, himself a committed socialist, places it carefully to expose the bankruptcy of the capitalist worldview. In Birling's lexicon, 'socialist' and 'crank' are synonyms; to believe in collective responsibility is to be insane. This conflation is the intellectual armour that protects him from the Inspector's message. Priestley uses the noun to force the audience to choose: is the Inspector a crank for insisting that we are 'members of one body', or is Birling a moral imbecile for denying it? The word also serves as a key to the play's 1945 context, reminding a post-war audience that the pre-war arrogance embodied by Birling had led, through selfishness and 'fire and blood and anguish', to catastrophe.
ex_en: When Birling spat out the word 'socialist', he was not describing an economic theory but naming his deepest fear: a world in which a man might be held responsible for the suffering of a stranger.
ex_zh: The word 'socialist' hung in Birling's mouth like a piece of spoiled meat, something he had to spit out before it poisoned the pure air of his drawing room.

def_en: grimly mocking or cynical; showing a dark, scornful humour
def_zh: sardonic smile, look sardonic, sardonic amusement
quote_en: "Inspector: ... (He makes a move as if concluding the session, possibly shutting up notebook, etc. Then surveys them sardonically.) No, I don't think any of you will forget. Nor that young man, Croft, though he at least had some affection for her and made her happy for a time." – Having made his final speech about collective responsibility, the Inspector surveys the shattered family with a dark, knowing amusement before walking out (Act 3)
quote_zh: The stage direction 'sardonically' transforms the Inspector from a detective into something closer to a prophet or a Fury. Sardonic humour—named, according to ancient belief, after a Sardinian plant that made men grimace before dying—is not light or kind; it is the laughter of someone who has seen too much to pretend any longer. Priestley grants the Inspector this look just before his exit to underline that, for all his talk of fire and blood and anguish, he is not hopeful about the Birlings' capacity to change. The sardonic survey is a moral verdict delivered without words: 'I have shown you the truth; now watch as you bury it again.' The fact that Gerald is singled out for partial praise only sharpens the mockery, reminding the others that the man they most respected was the one who, at least, felt some flicker of genuine feeling.
ex_en: The Inspector's sardonic glance, sweeping the family one last time, was the grim punctuation mark on a sentence that had begun with port and ended with a corpse.
ex_zh: His gaze, sardonic as a gargoyle's grin on a cathedral wall, mocked every prayer for forgiveness that had not yet been spoken and never would be.

def_en: the quality of being large, solid, and heavy, especially in a way that conveys authority
def_zh: impression of massiveness, sheer massiveness, physical massiveness
quote_en: "The inspector need not be a big man but he creates at once an impression of massiveness, solidity and purposefulness." – The stage directions describing Inspector Goole's entrance, which instantly changes the atmosphere of the dining room before he has even spoken (Act 1)
quote_zh: Priestley's choice of 'massiveness'—an abstraction normally reserved for architecture or sculpture—elevates the Inspector from an ordinary policeman to a symbolic force. The word suggests not physical bulk but moral weight, an immovable presence that will dominate the slender, brittle Birlings. The pairing with 'solidity' and 'purposefulness' creates a trinity of qualities that the family conspicuously lacks: they are, by contrast, flimsy, shifting, and self-deceiving. Priestley's instruction that the Inspector 'need not be a big man' underscores the point: his massiveness is metaphysical, the pressure of truth itself entering the room. This initial description functions as a promise to the audience that whatever follows will not be a polite conversation but a structural collapse.
ex_en: The Inspector's massiveness of presence meant that the Birling family, for all their furniture and wealth, seemed suddenly weightless and insubstantial against the gravity of a dead girl's story.
ex_zh: Massiveness gathered around the Inspector like an unseen cloak of stone, so that every chair in the room seemed to lean towards him as if tipping into a well of truth.

def_en: the quality of having or showing determination or resolve towards a specific aim
def_zh: with purposefulness, grim purposefulness, unshakable purposefulness
quote_en: "The inspector need not be a big man but he creates at once an impression of massiveness, solidity and purposefulness." – Same stage directions as above, listing the qualities that define the Inspector's entrance into the comfortable world of the Birlings (Act 1)
quote_zh: 'Purposefulness' is the active principle within the trinity that describes the Inspector. While 'massiveness' suggests passive weight and 'solidity' denotes permanence, 'purposefulness' is the directed energy that will drive the inquiry forward. The word implies that every question the Inspector asks, every pause he makes, is part of a design that the family cannot yet perceive. Priestley contrasts this quality with the random, self-serving impulses of the Birlings: Birling's greed, Sheila's vanity, Eric's lust, Mrs Birling's snobbery. The Inspector's purposefulness is moral teleology in human form—a force that knows where it is going because it is aligned with truth. It also dramatises Priestley's socialist conviction that a life without purpose beyond profit is not only shallow but dangerous.
ex_en: The Inspector's purposefulness turned the dining room into a courtroom, not through legal authority but through the sheer, unswerving direction of his questions towards the one thing the Birlings could not evade.
ex_zh: Purposefulness radiated from him like a compass needle pointing not north but towards the exact moment when every lie would collapse under its own weight.

def_en: causing one to feel unsettled, confused, or thrown off balance
def_zh: disconcerting habit, find it disconcerting, most disconcerting
quote_en: "He speaks carefully, weightily, and has a disconcerting habit of looking hard at the person he addresses before actually speaking." – The stage directions elaborate on the Inspector's unsettling manner, specifying the technique that will unnerve each family member in turn (Act 1)
quote_zh: 'Disconcerting' is a minor-key adjective that belies its destructive power. Derived from the French *déconcerter* (to throw into confusion), the word captures the Inspector's technique of destabilising the Birlings not through shouting but through silence and scrutiny. The habit of looking hard before speaking reverses the expected rhythm of social interaction: the Inspector claims the right to examine, to judge, before he utters a syllable. In a household built on surface politeness, this silent prelude is an act of psychological warfare. Priestley chooses 'disconcerting' rather than 'frightening' or 'aggressive' to emphasize that the threat is not physical but existential—the Birlings are being forced to see themselves as others might see them, and the experience is profoundly unsettling.
ex_en: The Inspector's disconcerting stare stripped away every layer of small talk until the characters found themselves, to their own horror, confessing truths they had hidden even from themselves.
ex_zh: His gaze, disconcerting as a mirror in a dark room, caught each of them in the act of pretending and held them there until the pretence crumbled.

def_en: extreme physical or mental suffering; intense pain
def_zh: in great agony, agony of, prolonged agony
quote_en: "Inspector: Yes, she was in great agony. They did everything they could for her at the infirmary, but she died. Suicide, of course. Eric: (involuntarily) My god!" – The Inspector describes Eva Smith's final hours with unflinching detail, and Eric reacts with a spontaneous outburst of horror (Act 1)
quote_zh: 'Agony' is one of the most physically jarring words in the play, and Priestley places it early to ensure the audience never forgets the human cost behind the moral arithmetic. Derived from the Greek *agonia* (a struggle for victory in the games, later extended to mental struggle and suffering), the word carries the weight of a body locked in combat with death. Eric's involuntary cry—'My god!'—is the only appropriate response to such language, and it marks him from the start as emotionally porous where his father is sealed. Priestley contrasts the agony of Eva's death with the comfort of the Birlings' dinner, a juxtaposition that indicts not only the family's actions but the entire social order that permits such suffering to go unseen and unrelieved.
ex_en: The Inspector's mention of Eva's agony transformed the dinner party from a celebration into an inquest, with each course now a reproach and every sip of port a silent toast to a girl they had all, in their different ways, helped to kill.
ex_zh: Agony seeped into the room like the smell of disinfectant from the infirmary, invisible but so penetrating that by the end every character was choking on it.

def_en: anxious or unsettled; thrown into a state of agitation
def_zh: exchange perturbed glances, look perturbed, visibly perturbed
quote_en: "Birling: (cutting in) Just a minute, Sheila. Now, inspector, perhaps you and I had better go and talk this over quietly in a corner- - Sheila: (cutting in) Why should you? He's finished with you. He says it's one of us now. // the other four exchange bewildered and perturbed glances.//" – After Sheila insists that the Inspector has moved beyond questioning her father, the family and Gerald look at each other in confusion and rising fear (Act 1)
quote_zh: The stage direction 'perturbed' moves the family's emotional temperature from intellectual confusion ('bewildered') to emotional distress. The word derives from the Latin *perturbare* (to disturb greatly), and it suggests an agitation that is not yet panic but is rapidly heading in that direction. Priestley uses this pairing of adjectives to chart the progressive collapse of the Birlings' composure: they are bewildered because the rules have changed, perturbed because they sense danger. Crucially, all four of them—Birling, Mrs Birling, Gerald, and Eric—share this moment of collective anxiety, a rare instant of unity that will soon dissolve under the pressure of individual interrogation. The word also foreshadows the deeper disturbance each of them will feel when their own complicity is exposed.
ex_en: The perturbed glances that flew between the four of them were the visible symptoms of a disease that had been incubating for two years, and for which the Inspector was now administering the only possible cure: confession.
ex_zh: Perturbation rippled across the room like wind over a still pond, distorting every familiar reflection until the family could no longer recognise themselves in the trembling water.

def_en: feelings of doubt or hesitation regarding the morality of a proposed action; moral principles
def_zh: have no scruples, elaborate scruples, scruples and fine feelings
quote_en: "Mrs Birling: She was claiming elaborate fine feelings and scruples that were simply absurd in a girl in her position. Inspector: (very sternly) Her position now is that she lies with a burnt- out inside on a ... I'm losing all patience with you people. What did she say?" – Mrs Birling recounts her interview with the pregnant girl who appealed for charity, mocking the moral scruples that prevented the girl from taking stolen money (Act 2)
quote_zh: 'Scruples' is the moral heart of the play, and Mrs Birling's sneer at the word exposes her own spiritual deformity. Derived from the Latin *scrupulus* (a small sharp stone), a scruple is something that pricks the conscience—a tiny but persistent discomfort that prevents wrongdoing. Eva Smith, penniless and desperate, possessed scruples that Mrs Birling, secure in her wealth and status, could not even recognise as morality. The adjective 'elaborate' with which Mrs Birling dismisses them is itself a confession of her own impoverishment: she believes that the poor have no right to fine moral distinctions. Priestley uses this moment to invert the expected hierarchy: the 'girl of that class' is revealed to be the ethical superior of the magistrate's wife.
ex_en: Mrs Birling's mockery of Eva's scruples was the moment when her own moral bankruptcy became undeniable: she could not see that a refusal to take stolen money, even in desperation, was a nobility she herself had never been required to demonstrate.
ex_zh: Scruples, those tiny stones in the shoe of conscience, had tripped Eva Smith on her path to survival, but Mrs Birling walked over them as if they were merely the whining of an inferior pavement.

def_en: suppressed, held back, or prevented from expressing freely
def_zh: stifled sob, stifled cry, stifled emotion
quote_en: "She looks at it closely, recognizes it with a little cry, gives a half- stifled sob, and then runs out." – After the Inspector shows her the photograph of Eva Smith, Sheila's recognition is immediate and visceral; she flees the room, unable to contain her distress (Act 1)
quote_zh: The stage direction 'half-stifled sob' captures the exact moment when Sheila's carefully maintained social poise collapses. A sob that is only half-stifled is one that the body forces out despite the mind's desperate effort to suppress it; the adjective reveals that Sheila's distress is too powerful for her manners to control. Priestley uses this physical detail to distinguish Sheila from her parents: she cannot, like her father, dismiss Eva's death as a 'horrid business' because she feels it in her lungs and throat. The half-stifled quality of the sob also suggests that her conscience has been half-stifled for two years, and the photograph is the catalyst that releases what she has been suppressing. This moment marks her as the character capable of genuine moral growth.
ex_en: Sheila's half-stifled sob was the only honest sound in the room since the Inspector's arrival, a wordless confession that broke through the bars of self-control and signalled that she, at least, would not escape into denials.
ex_zh: The sob, half-stifled, emerged from Sheila like a bird trapped in a closed room, beating its wings against the window of her composure until she could do nothing but flee.

def_en: the use of irony to mock or convey contempt; bitter, wounding wit
def_zh: sharp sarcasm, note of sarcasm, with sarcasm
quote_en: "Sheila: (with sharp sarcasm) of course not. You were the wonderful fairy prince. You must have adored it, Gerald. Gerald: all right - I did for a time. Nearly any man would have done. Sheila: that's probably about the best thing you've said tonight. At least it's honest." – After Gerald admits his affair with Daisy Renton, Sheila's bitterness surfaces in a voice laced with sarcasm, cutting through his attempt at self-justification (Act 2)
quote_zh: 'Sarcasm' comes from the Greek *sarkazein*, meaning to tear flesh, and Sheila's tone here is true to the etymology. Her words flay Gerald with savage precision, turning his own pretensions to generosity into the fairy-tale silliness of a 'wonderful fairy prince'. Priestley gives Sheila this weapon not to diminish her but to demonstrate that her moral awakening includes a new capacity for unvarnished speech. The sarcasm is both damaging and cleansing: it destroys the romantic myth of Gerald's affair, but it also strips away the gentlemanly evasions that have protected him. When Gerald responds with honesty—'nearly any man would have done'—Sheila's sarcasm relents, acknowledging that the crude truth is preferable to a beautiful lie.
ex_en: Sheila's sarcasm, born of fresh pain and long-suppressed suspicion, was the acid that burned through Gerald's smooth narrative and revealed the ordinary, unheroic selfishness beneath.
ex_zh: Sarcasm hung in her throat like a blade she could swallow or spit out, and when she chose to let it fly, it lodged in Gerald's chest with a precision that no amount of apology could dislodge.

def_en: to force or oblige someone to do something, often under moral or legal pressure
def_zh: compel him to confess, be compelled, ought to be compelled
quote_en: "Mrs Birling: ...so I told her it was her business to make him responsible. If he refused to marry her - - and in my opinion he ought to be compelled to - - then he must at least support her. Inspector: and what did she reply to that?" – Mrs Birling recounts her advice to the pregnant Eva Smith, demanding that the unknown father of the child be legally and morally forced into taking responsibility (Act 2)
quote_zh: 'Compelled' is the word that will return to destroy Mrs Birling. It carries the full weight of legal and social coercion, and she deploys it with the unthinking confidence of a magistrate's wife who believes the state exists to enforce her moral judgments. Priestley's dramatic irony here is almost unbearable: Mrs Birling demands that the father be compelled, little knowing that she is describing her own son, and that her earlier refusal to help the girl has already compelled her towards suicide. The word also taps into the play's central meditation on free will and accountability: can goodness be compelled, or must it arise from conscience? Mrs Birling's reliance on compulsion rather than compassion is the clearest symptom of a morality that has forgotten how to love.
ex_en: Mrs Birling's insistence that the father be compelled to marry made it impossible, when the truth about Eric emerged, for her to claim she had acted without malice; she had demanded compulsion for others but excused her own family from every law.
ex_zh: The word 'compel' fell from her lips like a judge's gavel, its echo still ringing when the door opened and the defendant walked in wearing her son's face.

def_en: deeply or moodily thoughtful; dwelling on a troubling subject
def_zh: sit brooding, brooding silence, brooding over
quote_en: "Sheila is still quietly crying. Mrs Birling has collapsed into a chair. Eric is brooding desperately. Birling, the only active one, hears the front door slam, moves hesitatingly towards the door..." – After the Inspector's exit, the family fragments: Sheila weeps, Mrs Birling collapses, and Eric sinks into a desperate, motionless contemplation of his guilt (Act 3)
quote_zh: The stage direction 'brooding desperately' captures Eric's state after the full horror of what he has done—and what his mother did—becomes irreversible. 'Brooding' evokes the image of a bird sitting heavily on its nest, but here the nest is guilt, and the egg that will hatch from it is despair. The adverb 'desperately' intensifies the image: this is not philosophical melancholy but the paralysed, hollow-eyed stare of a young man who has seen his own soul and found it corrupt. Priestley contrasts Eric's brooding with Birling's busy, shallow activity (pouring a drink, checking the door) to show that the older generation refuses to sit still with their shame. Eric's brooding, painful though it is, is a sign that he has not escaped into denial, and that a moral reckoning, however slow, may yet begin.
ex_en: Eric's brooding, wordless and immobile, was the dark mirror of his mother's bustling self-justification, reflecting a guilt that no committee speech could cover.
ex_zh: Brooding settled over Eric like a low winter sky, grey and oppressive, pressing his thoughts down into the frozen mud of everything he could never undo.

def_en: in a manner that shows a strong desire to own, control, or guard someone or something
def_zh: gaily possessively, speak possessively, look possessively
quote_en: "Sheila: (gaily, possessively) I should jolly well think not, Gerald, I'd hate you to know all about port - like one of these purple- faced old men." – After Gerald admits he is no judge of port, Sheila teases him with a mixture of affection and ownership, playfully marking her territory (Act 1)
quote_zh: The stage direction 'possessively' reveals a crucial dimension of Sheila before the Inspector arrives. Her playful claim over Gerald—'I'd hate you to know all about port'—is not merely light- hearted fiancée banter but a territorial assertion of ownership. This word foreshadows the insecurity that will later erupt when she learns of Gerald's affair with Daisy Renton. Priestley uses the adverb to indicate that Sheila's engagement is partly defined by a desire to possess, a quality that mirrors her father's possessive attitude toward his business and reputation. The irony is that Sheila's possessiveness, though harmless in this early exchange, is psychologically adjacent to the selfishness that will lead her to demand Eva Smith's dismissal at Milwards. Her arc from possessiveness to moral awakening is one of the play's most significant trajectories.
ex_en: Sheila's possessive affection for Gerald, charming at the dinner table, contained within it the seed of the jealous fury that would destroy Eva Smith's chance at a respectable job.
ex_zh: Possessiveness curled around her words like ivy around a young tree, decorative and green, hiding how tightly it was squeezing the trunk beneath.

def_en: to smile radiantly and warmly
def_zh: beam at, beam with pleasure, beam proudly
quote_en: "Birling beams at them and clearly relaxes." – After the maid has left and the port glasses are filled, Birling surveys his family and Gerald with self- satisfied warmth, ready to deliver his paternal wisdom (Act 1)
quote_zh: Priestley's choice of the verb 'beam' transforms Birling momentarily into a figure of benign, almost foolish, contentment. A beam is a ray of light, and Birling radiates pleasure not because of any achievement of his own making but because the social order has confirmed his place in it. The verb suggests an unguarded, almost childlike satisfaction, which makes the subsequent intrusion of the Inspector all the more devastating. Priestley juxtaposes Birling's beaming relaxation with the 'sharp ring of a door bell' that interrupts his speech a few lines later. The beam, warm and intimate, is extinguished by the hard light the Inspector demands, and the stage direction 'beams' becomes a memory of a complacency that will never return.
ex_en: Birling's beaming face, lit by port and paternal pride, was a snapshot of a man who believed that the universe existed to confirm his own good fortune.
ex_zh: The beam that spread across Birling's face was a brief, golden illusion—a candle flame that guttered the instant the outer door opened and the draught of truth swept in.

def_en: the act of pressing a point, demand, or request persistently
