def_zh: with insistence, gentle insistence, repeated insistence
quote_en: "Gerald: (smiling) Wouldn't dream of it. In fact, I insist upon being one of the family now. I've been trying long enough, haven't I? (as she does not reply, with more insistence.) Haven't I? You know I have." – Gerald, in front of the whole family, presses Sheila for affirmation of his long- standing courtship, the stage direction marking his unease when she does not immediately respond (Act 1)
quote_zh: Gerald's 'insistence' is a small but telling crack in his polished gentleman's facade. Priestley uses the noun to capture a moment of social pressure: Gerald demands that Sheila publicly confirm her affection, not because he doubts it but because he needs the performance to be complete. The stage direction 'with more insistence' suggests that Gerald's charm has limits; when teased or delayed, he becomes slightly coercive, a trait that will reappear in his account of how he 'insisted' on Daisy Renton moving into Morgan Terrace. The word thus links the drawing- room Gerald with the man who used his wealth and position to make a desperate girl dependent on him. Insistence, Priestley suggests, can be dressed up as love but is often merely the exercise of power.
ex_en: Gerald's insistence on being acknowledged as 'one of the family' was the first indication that his gentlemanly ease could harden into something more demanding when his wishes were not immediately gratified.
ex_zh: The insistence in his voice was velvet- wrapped iron, a soft but unyielding pressure that nudged Sheila towards the answer he wanted as surely as a hand guides a tiller.

def_en: a person or entity competing with another for the same objective or superiority
def_zh: friendly rival, business rival, rival firm
quote_en: "Birling: Your father and I have been friendly rivals in business for some time now - though Crofts Limited are both older and bigger than Birling and Company - and now you've brought us together, and perhaps we may look forward to the time when Crofts and Birlings are no longer competing but are working together - for lower costs and higher prices." – Birling, toasting the engagement of Sheila and Gerald, cannot resist turning a family celebration into a business calculation, openly dreaming of a merger (Act 1)
quote_zh: 'Rivals' is the word that betrays the commercial heart beating beneath Birling's paternal toast. In a speech purporting to welcome Gerald as a future son- in- law, Birling immediately reframes the marriage as a business opportunity. The adjective 'friendly' is a thin veneer over a relationship defined entirely by competition and profit, and the vision of 'lower costs and higher prices' is a brutally honest summary of capitalist ambition. Priestley uses the noun to expose how thoroughly market logic has colonised Birling's emotional life: even his daughter's happiness is assessed in terms of its impact on the balance sheet. The word foreshadows the Inspector's revelation that Birling's treatment of Eva Smith was driven by the same logic of cost- cutting and competitive ruthlessness.
ex_en: Birling's speech about friendly rivals revealed a mind so saturated in business that even the engagement toast became a prospectus for a corporate merger.
ex_zh: The word 'rival' sat at the dinner table like an uninvited guest, smiling politely while everyone pretended the flowers were not from a funeral.

def_en: tending to see the worst aspect of things or believe the worst will happen
def_zh: pessimistic talk, unduly pessimistic, pessimistic outlook
quote_en: "Birling: There's a good deal of silly talk about these days - but - and I speak as a hard- headed business man, who has to take risks and know what he's about – I say, you can ignore all this silly pessimistic talk." – Birling, holding forth after dinner, dismisses those who forecast labour troubles, war, and social unrest as foolish pessimists (Act 1)
quote_zh: 'Pessimistic' functions as Birling's verbal dismissal of any perspective that challenges his own interests. To label talk of strikes, war, and economic instability as 'pessimistic' is to refuse to engage with reality on any terms other than those that serve profit. Priestley loads the adjective with dramatic irony: the audience of 1945 knows that the 'pessimistic talk' about war was entirely accurate, that the Titanic—which Birling will cite a moment later as proof of progress—will sink within days, and that labour agitation will reshape British society. Birling's confident rejection of 'pessimism' thus becomes the play's most sustained illustration of wilful blindness, a state of mind that will later prevent him from grasping the moral weight of his actions against Eva Smith.
ex_en: Birling's charge of pessimism against anyone who predicted disaster was the rhetorical armour of a man whose entire identity depended on the world continuing to run exactly as it had always done.
ex_zh: Pessimistic words fluttered around Birling's ears like moths around a porch light, and he swatted them away with the same blithe certainty that darkness would never actually arrive.

def_en: the title of the German Emperor, Wilhelm II, who ruled during the period leading up to the First World War
def_zh: the Kaiser, Kaiser Wilhelm, Kaiser's speech
quote_en: "Birling: Just because the Kaiser makes a speech or two, or a few German officers have too much to drink and begin talking nonsense, you'll hear some people say that war's inevitable. And to that I say – fiddlesticks!" – Birling dismisses the threat of war by trivialising the political tensions in Europe, reducing them to the bluster of a few intoxicated officers (Act 1)
quote_zh: 'Kaiser' is the pivot on which Priestley's dramatic irony turns most savagely. Birling names the German Emperor only to reduce the figurehead of an entire military empire to a man who merely 'makes a speech or two', a rhetorical move that strips geopolitical tensions of all seriousness. The 1945 audience, still bearing the scars of two world wars, would hear this dismissal with a kind of horrified recognition: the man who cannot take the Kaiser seriously is the man who cannot see the rising storm. Priestley uses the title to signal a catastrophic failure of historical imagination, a failure that is part of the same selfish blindness that prevents Birling from understanding his responsibility to Eva Smith. The Kaiser is the global equivalent of the petty capitalist who cannot imagine that a wage dispute might lead to a suicide.
ex_en: Birling's dismissal of the Kaiser as a harmless speechifier crystallised his fatal inability to take any threat seriously unless it appeared on his own balance sheet.
ex_zh: The word 'Kaiser' fell from Birling's mouth as if it were a provincial alderman's name and not the fuse of a bomb whose blast would bury an entire generation.

def_en: an expression of dismissive contempt, meaning nonsense or rubbish
def_zh: utter fiddlesticks, say fiddlesticks
quote_en: "Birling: ...you'll hear some people say that war's inevitable. And to that I say – fiddlesticks! The Germans don't want war. Nobody wants war, except some half- civilized folks in the Balkans." – Birling's confident dismissal of the possibility of war, delivered with a wave of the hand and a trivialising exclamation (Act 1)
quote_zh: 'Fiddlesticks' is a word of almost comical inadequacy, and Priestley puts it in Birling's mouth to damn him. The exclamation belongs to the nursery, to petty domestic annoyance; it is what one says to a child who has made a silly mistake, not to the prospect of millions of deaths. By choosing this childish interjection, Priestley forces the audience to measure the gulf between Birling's tone—flippant, self- assured, condescending—and the subject matter. The word is a linguistic embodiment of the very blindness the play condemns: Birling cannot speak of impending catastrophe in an adult register because he has no adult capacity for imagining catastrophe. The fiddlestick is both a bow for a violin and, in the plural, a synonym for trivial nonsense, and Birling, by uttering it, becomes a fiddler while Europe burns.
ex_en: Birling's 'fiddlesticks' was not an argument but an evasion, a word with which he brushed away the most terrible century in human history as if it were a fly on his waistcoat.
ex_zh: Fiddlesticks!—and with that one syllable, the future was cancelled, the trenches were filled with flowers, and the world continued to revolve around a dining table in Brumley.

def_en: a person who spreads frightening rumours or exaggerates dangers in order to stir up fear
def_zh: a scaremonger, listen to scaremongers
quote_en: "Birling: ...and not a few German officers taking nonsense and a few scaremongers here making a fuss about nothing." – Birling concludes his lecture on the impossibility of war by dismissing all contrary voices as anxious troublemakers (Act 1)
quote_zh: 'Scaremonger' is Birling's ultimate insult, a label he applies to anyone whose understanding of the world is based on evidence rather than self- interest. A monger is a dealer, a petty trader, and the compound word carries the contempt of a successful capitalist for those who peddle in fear rather than goods. The irony is that the scaremongers Birling despises were, in 1912, telling the truth about German militarism, while Birling, the self- proclaimed realist, was peddling a fantasy of endless prosperity. Priestley uses the noun to dramatise a perennially dangerous human tendency: the dismissal of accurate warnings by those whose comfort depends on not hearing them. The word also links to the play's central theme: the Birlings have spent their lives scaremongering about the poor, about socialists, about anyone who questions their right to profit.
ex_en: The scaremongers Birling mocked in 1912 were the historians of 1918, their warnings vindicated by the trenches while his own reassurances lay buried under Flanders mud.
ex_zh: Scaremongers, Birling called them, not realising that some monsters are real and that the only difference between a scaremonger and a prophet is the date on the newspaper.

def_en: (British informal) trivial, silly, or foolish; obsessed about something
def_zh: potty about, go potty, a bit potty
quote_en: "Eric: ...I left'em talking about clothes again. You'd think a girl had never any clothes before she gets married. Women are potty about 'em." – After the ladies have retired, Eric rejoins the men and dismisses the conversation in the drawing room with a casual, dismissive remark about female frivolity (Act 1)
quote_zh: Eric's use of 'potty' is a small but significant piece of characterisation. The adjective means both 'foolishly obsessed' and 'slightly mad', and Eric applies it to the women's conversation with a mixture of affectionate condescension and genuine incomprehension. His father, in response, will deliver a surprisingly thoughtful observation about clothes meaning 'something quite different to a woman', but Eric's 'potty' reveals a young man who has not yet learned to respect the interior lives of women. This casual dismissiveness will reappear in his treatment of Eva Smith, whom he uses for a 'stupid drunken evening' and then forgets. The word is a miniature prefiguration of the sexual irresponsibility that will be exposed by the Inspector.
ex_en: Eric's casual dismissal of female conversation as 'potty' betrayed the same unthinking condescension that would later make it possible for him to take what he wanted from Eva Smith and then walk away.
ex_zh: The word 'potty' fluttered out of Eric's mouth like a scrap of wrapping paper, careless and soon forgotten, while the gift it had covered lay smashed beneath the tree.

def_en: an eccentric person given to holding views that others regard as strange or impractical, especially about politics or society
def_zh: cranks and socialists, listen to cranks, dismissed as a crank
quote_en: "Birling: ...But the way some of these cranks talk and write now, you'd think everybody has to look after everybody else, as if we were all mixed up together like bees in a hive - community and all that nonsense." – Immediately before the Inspector's arrival, Birling lays out his philosophy of individualism, dismissing advocates of social responsibility as eccentric troublemakers (Act 1)
quote_zh: 'Crank' is the word Birling uses to describe what the play invites the audience to recognise as a moral vision. The term reduces socialists, trade unionists, and advocates for the poor to figures of ridicule—eccentrics who can be safely ignored by sensible businessmen. Priestley's timing is impeccable: within moments of this speech, the doorbell rings and the 'crank' who enters turns out to be the only character with genuine moral clarity. The word 'crank' thus becomes a trap that Birling sets for himself; by dismissing the philosophy of collective responsibility as the raving of cranks, he makes himself the fool when the Inspector demonstrates that philosophy's absolute, devastating truth. The metaphor that follows—'bees in a hive'—is, for Priestley's socialist audience, an image not of 'nonsense' but of ideal community.
ex_en: Birling's dismissal of socialists as 'cranks' backfired the instant the Inspector walked through the door, for the 'crank' proved to possess the only wisdom in the room worth heeding.
ex_zh: Cranks, Birling called them, these men who thought we were all connected, and he laughed at the hive until the moment the bee stung his own throat.

def_en: deeply shocked or astonished; so amazed that one is momentarily thrown off balance
def_zh: staggered by, look staggered, utterly staggered
quote_en: "Mrs Birling: (staggered) it isn't true. You know him, Gerald - and you're a man - you must know it isn't true." – After Sheila insists that Eric has been drinking heavily for two years, Mrs Birling reels in disbelief and appeals to Gerald for masculine corroboration that her son cannot possibly have such a habit (Act 2)
quote_zh: Priestley's stage direction 'staggered' transforms Mrs Birling from the composed magistrate's wife into a woman who has just suffered a physical blow. The word suggests a momentary loss of equilibrium, the body registering a truth before the mind can accept it. Mrs Birling's appeal to Gerald as 'a man' betrays her reliance on gender as a moral category; she assumes that male testimony will restore the order her daughter has disrupted. The adjective also foreshadows the far greater stagger to come when she learns that the unknown father she has condemned is Eric himself. Priestley uses this small physical reaction to mark the first hairline crack in her formidable self-assurance, a crack that will widen into the final shattering revelation.
ex_en: Mrs Birling's staggered disbelief at the truth about Eric's drinking was a preview of the complete moral collapse she would experience when the full extent of her own cruelty became undeniable.
ex_zh: The truth hit Mrs Birling like a fist to the breastbone, and for one staggered moment she stood utterly still, a statue with a cracking pedestal.

def_en: having the eyes wide open and staring, especially in a foolish or lecherous manner
def_zh: stare goggle-eyed, goggle-eyed with drink, goggle-eyed old man
quote_en: "Gerald: Old Joe Meggarty, half- drunk and goggle- eyed, had wedged her into a corner with that obscene fat carcass of his-" – Gerald describes his first encounter with Daisy Renton, cornered in the Palace Bar by a lecherous alderman too drunk to control his stare (Act 2)
quote_zh: The compound adjective 'goggle-eyed' paints Alderman Meggarty as both grotesque and predatory. 'Goggle' suggests eyes that protrude unnaturally, fixed on their target with a mindless, amphibian hunger. Combined with 'half-drunk', the phrase strips away any residual dignity from a public figure who, in the daylight world of Brumley, commands respect as a magistrate. Priestley uses this vivid, almost cartoonish description to do serious moral work: he exposes the rot beneath civic respectability. The man who sits on the bench with Birling is, in the bar, a 'goggle-eyed' predator, and the contrast indicts the entire social order that protects such men.
ex_en: Alderman Meggarty, goggle-eyed and reeking of drink, was the living refutation of Birling's claim that 'sound useful party men' were the pillars of a decent society.
ex_zh: Goggle-eyed and slack-jawed, Meggarty stared at Daisy Renton as if she were a cut of meat hanging in a butcher's window and he had forgotten his wallet but not his appetite.

def_en: the dead body of an animal; used contemptuously to describe a gross or obscenely fat human body
def_zh: fat carcass, obscene carcass, rotting carcass
quote_en: "Gerald: ...had wedged her into a corner with that obscene fat carcass of his-" – Gerald's disgusted description of Alderman Meggarty's body as he pinned Daisy Renton in the bar, using a word normally reserved for dead animals (Act 2)
quote_zh: Gerald's choice of 'carcass' reduces Alderman Meggarty to dead meat. It is a word of visceral revulsion, stripping the alderman of his humanity and leaving only the physical, decaying matter of a body. Priestley puts this image in Gerald's mouth not to celebrate his sensitivity but to underline his hypocrisy: Gerald recoils from Meggarty's drunken predation, yet he himself will later install the same woman in a flat and use her for his own pleasure. The word 'carcass' thus functions on two levels: it condemns the lechery it literally describes while also hinting, through Gerald's own eventual fall, that the distinction between the predator and the 'well-bred young man-about-town' is thinner than either would wish to admit.
ex_en: Gerald's disgust at Meggarty's 'obscene fat carcass' was a convenient moral shudder that allowed him to forget, for a moment, that his own treatment of Daisy Renton had been merely a more polished form of predation.
ex_zh: The word 'carcass' dropped from Gerald's lips like a piece of butcher's offal, meant to disgust the listener but failing to conceal the blood on his own manicured hands.

def_en: a man who engages in numerous casual sexual relationships with women, typically with a predatory or exploitative edge
def_zh: notorious womanizer, inveterate womanizer
quote_en: "Gerald: He's a notorious womanizer as well as being one of the worst sots and rogues in Brumley- - Inspector: Quite right." – Gerald, identifying Alderman Meggarty, adds to the catalogue of his vices the specific charge of sexual predation, a charge the Inspector immediately confirms (Act 2)
quote_zh: 'Womanizer' is a word that bridges the gap between private vice and public knowledge. Gerald pronounces it with the confidence of someone who knows the truth about Brumley's elite, and the Inspector's flat endorsement—'Quite right'—gives the accusation the weight of official knowledge. Priestley uses the noun to reveal that the sexual exploitation of women was not a secret in Edwardian society; it was simply a secret that no one in power acted upon. The word also rebounds on Gerald himself: his own behaviour with Daisy Renton, however wrapped in the silk of respectability, fits the pattern of a man who uses his position to secure sexual favours and then moves on. The womanizer and the respectable fiancé are, in Priestley's moral accounting, uncomfortably close.
ex_en: Alderman Meggarty, the 'notorious womanizer', was merely Gerald's future reflected in a grimy pub mirror, the gentleman's charm stripped of its polish to reveal the same old appetite.
ex_zh: They called him a womanizer in a tone that mixed envy with reproach, as if his sins were just the over-ripened fruit of a tree they all wished they could climb.

def_en: a habitual drunkard; someone who is chronically and conspicuously drunk
def_zh: worst sot, old sot, village sot
quote_en: "Gerald: He's a notorious womanizer as well as being one of the worst sots and rogues in Brumley-" – Gerald concludes his portrait of Alderman Meggarty by adding alcoholism to sexual predation, rounding out the picture of a man who embodies everything corrupt in the town's public life (Act 2)
quote_zh: 'Sot' is an old, contemptuous word, and Gerald's use of it places Meggarty in a line of Hogarthian grotesques. It is a term that strips away all dignity, leaving only the bloated, red-faced drunkard. Priestley places this noun alongside 'womanizer' and 'rogue' to build a triptych of respectable male vice: Meggarty drinks, he preys on women, and he cheats. The word is especially resonant because Eric's own drinking, which the family has been half-concealing all evening, will shortly be exposed as the source of his moral collapse. By having Gerald denounce Meggarty as a sot, Priestley sharpens the irony that will land on Eric, and on the whole family that judges others while excusing its own.
ex_en: The alderman, a renowned sot, stumbled through Brumley's corridors of power in a haze of whisky and privilege, never once called to account until a policeman who wasn't a policeman named him aloud.
ex_zh: Sots like Meggarty, Gerald seemed to say, belonged in the gutter, but the glass in his own hand was still half-full and the night was young.

def_en: the quality of being impudent; impertinent boldness, especially towards a social superior
def_zh: gross impudence, have the impudence, damned impudence
quote_en: "Birling: And I should think so! Damned impudence!" – After Mrs Birling reveals that the desperate girl who came to the committee had called herself 'Mrs Birling', Birling explodes with outrage at what he sees as an outrage against his family name (Act 2)
quote_zh: 'Impudence' is Birling's final, futile defence. The word denotes a breach of deference, an offence against the social ladder, and it reveals the real outrage in Birling's mind: not that a girl should starve or drink disinfectant, but that she should dare to borrow his name. Priestley gives Birling this explosion of wounded vanity at the same moment that the audience is absorbing the news of Eva's pregnancy, creating a moral contrast so sharp it cuts. The phrase 'Damned impudence' reduces the entire tragedy to a matter of etiquette; it is the same instinct that led Birling to call the Inspector 'officious'. Impudence, in Birling's vocabulary, is the only sin he can recognise, and his preoccupation with it blinds him to the real sins piling up at his door.
ex_en: Birling's cry of 'Damned impudence' summarised his entire moral philosophy: the worst crime a person could commit was not cruelty but a failure to know their place.
ex_zh: Impudence, Birling called it, as if the wrong name on a form carried more weight in the scales of justice than a life that had been starved, used, and finally poured down an infirmary drain.

def_en: in a manner that shows great satisfaction or victory; exultantly
def_zh: say triumphantly, look triumphantly, declare triumphantly
quote_en: "Mrs Birling: (triumphantly) I'm glad to hear it. Inspector: No hushing up, eh? Make an example of the young man, eh? Public confession of responsibility - um? Mrs Birling: Certainly. I consider it your duty." – Having demanded that the father of Eva's child be hunted down and publicly shamed, Mrs Birling believes she has won a moral victory and hands the Inspector his marching orders (Act 2)
quote_zh: The stage direction 'triumphantly' is a study in terrible, dramatic irony. Mrs Birling speaks the adverb with the full weight of a woman who thinks she has restored order, defended the moral law, and put an intrusive policeman in his place. The audience, already suspecting or knowing that Eric is the father, watches her triumph with mounting horror: every word she speaks is a nail in her own son's coffin. Priestley uses the word to demonstrate how the appetite for punishment can turn back on the punisher. Mrs Birling's triumph is the last moment of her old self; within minutes, the door will open and everything she has demanded for 'the young man' will fall on Eric. The adverb becomes a tombstone for her moral credibility.
ex_en: Mrs Birling's triumphant insistence that the father be made an example of was the final, unwitting stone she placed on the trapdoor beneath her own feet.
ex_zh: Triumphantly, she planted the flag of her righteousness on a hill of corpses, not yet realising the one body at the bottom wore her son's face.

def_en: in a sudden, violent outburst of emotion, typically anger
def_zh: say explosively, react explosively, explode explosively
quote_en: "Eric: (miserably) could I have a drink first? Birling: (explosively) No. Inspector: (firmly) Yes. (As Birling looks like interrupting explosively.) I know - he's your son and this is your house - but look at him. He needs a drink now just to see him through." – When Eric, broken and pale, returns and asks for whisky before confessing, Birling erupts with furious denial, only to be overruled by the Inspector (Act 3)
quote_zh: Priestley's 'explosively' captures the eruption of Birling's authoritarian rage at the moment of his son's greatest vulnerability. The adverb suggests a detonation, a sudden release of pressure that is more about control than morality. Birling's 'No' is not a considered judgment but a reflex of the punitive father who has lost all command. Moments earlier, Birling himself poured a drink to steady his own nerves, and the hypocrisy of denying Eric the same comfort is glaring. The Inspector's calm intervention—'Yes'—and his explanation that Eric 'needs a drink now just to see him through' reframes the whisky not as a vice but as a human necessity, a gesture of compassion that the father cannot offer. The word 'explosively' thus marks the final fracture between Birling's authoritarianism and the gentler justice the play endorses.
ex_en: Birling's explosively refused drink was the last act of a father who could command furniture and business ledgers but had no idea how to reach the suffering son standing three feet away.
ex_zh: Explosively, the word 'No' burst from Birling's throat, a bullet of rejection that would have shattered Eric entirely if the Inspector had not stepped into the line of fire.

def_en: foolish, outrageous, or amusing behaviour; clownish tricks or pranks
def_zh: the Inspector's antics, ridiculous antics, strange antics
quote_en: "Birling: I'll admit that fellow's antics rattled us a bit. But we've found him out - and all we have to do is to keep our heads. Now it's our turn." – After discovering the Inspector was not a real policeman, Birling belittles the entire interrogation as theatrical mischief and prepares to reassert control (Act 3)
quote_zh: 'Antics' is Birling's final attempt to shrink the Inspector's moral authority to the dimensions of a music-hall performance. The word suggests clowning, horseplay, the absurd gestures of a performer who can be laughed off stage. By labelling the interrogation 'antics', Birling erases the real confessions of theft, sexual exploitation, and cruelty that emerged during it. Priestley uses this dismissive noun to expose Birling's incorrigibility: even after confessing his own actions, he still cannot see that what happened was anything more than a 'bit of bluff'. The word is a verbal umbrella under which he can shelter his family from the rain of truth, and the audience watches him raise it knowing that the final telephone call is about to drench him beyond recovery.
ex_en: Birling's dismissal of the Inspector's visit as 'antics' proved that a man who has spent his life mocking the truth will not recognise it even when it sits at his own dinner table and speaks with his own voice.
ex_zh: Antics, Birling called them, those hours when the truth had crept into the room like a slow poison, and he, blind and laughing, had mistaken the symptoms for entertainment.

def_en: foolish or fanciful talk; nonsense; something without substance
def_zh: a lot of moonshine, sheer moonshine, talk moonshine
quote_en: "Birling: (triumphantly) There you are! Proof positive. The whole story's just a lot of moonshine. Nothing but an elaborate sell!" – After Gerald telephones the infirmary and learns that no girl has died that day, Birling crows over the apparent vindication, dismissing the entire evening's revelations as fantasy (Act 3)
quote_zh: 'Moonshine' is Birling's victory cry, and it is also his epitaph. The word, with its associations of pale, insubstantial light and illicitly distilled spirits, perfectly captures his refusal to face reality. Moonshine is what drunks see and fools believe, and by applying it to Eva Smith's story, Birling not only denies the truth but insults the very idea that a man like him could be connected to suffering. The audience, watching in 1945 and knowing that the whole structure of Birling's world will collapse within two years, hears the word as a terrible joke: the real 'moonshine' is Birling's belief in progress, prosperity, and the unsinkable ship. The term thus marks the exact point at which his moral blindness becomes indistinguishable from madness.
ex_en: Birling's relief that the evening's ordeal was all 'moonshine' lasted only as long as it took the telephone to ring again, its shrill bell the only real voice left in the room.
ex_zh: Moonshine, Birling whispered, as if the nightmare could be dissolved by a single word, but the word evaporated and the nightmare stayed, waiting patiently for the dial tone.

def_en: solid, large, and imposing in appearance; suggesting prosperity without genuine warmth
def_zh: substantial house, substantial furniture, substantial and comfortable
quote_en: "The general effect is a substantial and heavily comfortable but not cosy and homelike." – The opening stage directions describing the Birlings' dining room and its expensive but emotionally sterile atmosphere (Act 1)
quote_zh: Priestley's choice of 'substantial' in the initial stage directions is a deliberate piece of social commentary before a line of dialogue is spoken. The adjective denotes weight, solidity, and material prosperity—the furniture is 'good solid furniture of the period'—but the qualification that follows ('but not cosy and homelike') immediately undermines the impression of comfort. 'Substantial' thus becomes a synonym for the Birling family's moral condition: they possess the outward signs of success, but their substance is merely physical, not spiritual. The word foreshadows the Inspector's exposure of a household built on profit rather than love, where the heavy furniture cannot compensate for the lightness of their consciences. Priestley uses this adjective to invite the audience to distrust appearances from the very first moment.
ex_en: The 'substantial' dining room, with its heavy furniture and polished silver, was a monument to Birling's success and a mausoleum for the compassion he had never learned.
ex_zh: The substantial walls of the Birling house stood like a fortress against the world, their thick plaster muffling the cries that seeped in from the streets beyond.

def_en: warm, comfortable, and inviting; creating a feeling of intimacy and ease
def_zh: cosy atmosphere, cosy and homelike, cosy room
quote_en: "The general effect is a substantial and heavily comfortable but not cosy and homelike." – The opening stage directions that define the Birling dining room as a space of material comfort devoid of genuine domestic warmth (Act 1)
quote_zh: Priestley's negation—'not cosy and homelike'—is a more powerful description than any positive adjective could be. The word 'cosy' evokes snugness, intimacy, the sense of a family gathered in affectionate ease; by denying it, the stage directions establish that the Birlings' home is a performance space rather than a sanctuary. The 'heavily comfortable' furniture suggests upholstery purchased for show, not for curling up in. This absence of cosiness is the visual correlative of the family's emotional life: Arthur and Sybil Birling are incapable of the warmth and vulnerability that genuine cosiness requires. The Inspector will shatter even this chilly comfort, revealing that the home the Birlings have built rests on the suffering of those they have excluded from their heavily guarded warmth.
ex_en: The absence of cosiness in the Birling household was the absence of love itself, a void filled with port and protocol but never with the simple kindness that makes a house a home.
ex_zh: Cosiness, if it had ever knocked at the Birling door, would have been turned away by Edna with a polite 'Not at home', its muddy shoes an affront to the polished floor.

def_en: a member of a municipal council; a civic dignitary ranking below a mayor
def_zh: former alderman, alderman for years, serve as alderman
quote_en: "Birling: I was an alderman for years - and lord mayor two years ago - and I'm still on the bench - so I know the Brumley police offices pretty well - and I thought I'd never seen you before." – Birling asserts his civic credentials to the newly arrived Inspector, listing his titles as a shield against scrutiny (Act 1)
quote_zh: The title 'alderman' drips from Birling's tongue as the first weapon in his arsenal of self-importance. An alderman is a local councillor, a minor figure in the machinery of municipal government, but Birling inflates the role into a proof of his impregnability. The rhetorical sequence—alderman, lord mayor, magistrate—is a ladder he climbs in a single breath, each rung designed to put the Inspector in his place. Priestley uses this word to expose Birling's philosophy of status as immunity: he believes that civic honours exempt him from moral questioning. The irony is that these very titles—conferred by a society that also employs 'cheap labour'—implicate him more deeply in the system that destroyed Eva Smith. An alderman is supposed to serve the public; Birling has served only himself.
ex_en: Birling's boastful recitation of his aldermanic years was an attempt to build a fortress of titles around himself, forgetting that the Inspector's warrant was signed by a higher court than the Brumley town hall.
ex_zh: Alderman—the word puffed out his chest like a pigeon fanning its feathers, never suspecting that the cat was already in the dovecote.

def_en: done without conscious control; occurring as an automatic, unplanned reaction
def_zh: cry out involuntarily, gasp involuntarily, flinch involuntarily
quote_en: "Inspector: She'd been taken there this afternoon because she'd swallowed a lot of strong disinfectant. Burnt her inside out, of course. Eric: (involuntarily) My god!" – The Inspector describes Eva Smith's horrific death, and Eric reacts with a spontaneous, unguarded cry that breaks through the dinner-party decorum (Act 1)
quote_zh: The stage direction 'involuntarily' is the first genuine human response in the play. Up to this moment, every word has been chosen for social effect—Birling's boasts, Gerald's politeness, Mrs Birling's reproaches—but Eric's 'My god!' escapes him before his manners can intercept it. The adverb suggests a truth that the conscious mind would suppress: Eric has been touched, however briefly, by the reality of another's suffering. Priestley uses this involuntary reaction to distinguish Eric from his father, who a moment later will dismiss the death as a 'horrid business'. The word also prefigures the later confessions that the Inspector will extract, all of which are a form of involuntary truth-telling, a bypassing of the social ego's defences.
ex_en: Eric's involuntary cry was the first tear in the fabric of the family's composure, and through that small rip the entire weight of what they had done would eventually pour.
ex_zh: The word 'God' burst from Eric involuntarily, like a bubble of air from a drowning man's lungs, the last gasp of a conscience that had been underwater for years.

def_en: temporary living quarters; rented rooms, typically small and impersonal
def_zh: in her lodgings, cheap lodgings, back-street lodgings
quote_en: "Inspector: I've been round to the room she had, and she'd left a letter there and a sort of diary. Like a lot of these young women who get into various kinds of trouble, she'd used more than one name. But her original name - her real name - was Eva Smith. ... I found a photograph of her in her lodgings." – The Inspector explains his investigation, locating Eva Smith's life in the temporary, anonymous spaces of rented rooms (Act 1)
quote_zh: 'Lodgings' is a word that speaks of transience and impermanence. Unlike a home, lodgings are rented, provisional, the shelter of those who have no roots. Priestley uses this noun to define Eva Smith's existence as one of constant displacement—from the factory to Milwards to Morgan Terrace to the seaside to the room where the Inspector finds her photograph. The contrast with the Birlings' 'substantial' dining room is stark: they inhabit a house built on ownership and inheritance; she passed through rooms that never belonged to her. The photograph, a fragile record of a face, is the only permanent marker she leaves. 'Lodgings' thus becomes a metonym for the precarity of the working class, whose lives can be terminated by a dismissive employer or a cold committee and whose memorials are scraps of paper left behind in rented rooms.
ex_en: The Inspector's discovery of Eva's photograph in her lodgings was a quiet, devastating rebuke to every comfortable sitting room in Brumley that had never made space for the girl who had no other home.
ex_zh: Lodgings—the word tasted of damp wallpaper and other people's furniture, of keys that opened doors that never felt like your own, of a life lived in rooms that remembered every previous tenant's despair.

def_en: in a slyly playful or teasing manner, often with an undercurrent of knowingness
def_zh: remark archly, smile archly, glance archly
quote_en: "Sheila: Milwards! We go there - in fact, I was there this afternoon - (archly to Gerald) for your benefit." – When the Inspector mentions the shop where Eva Smith briefly worked, Sheila recollects her own visit with a teasing, self-satisfied glance at her fiancé, moments before she recognises the photograph (Act 1)
quote_zh: Priestley's stage direction 'archly' crystallises the particular quality of Sheila's pre-Inspector personality. The adverb suggests a performance of knowingness, a playful superiority that assumes Gerald is in on the joke. Her shopping trip was 'for his benefit', and the arch glance is a tiny act of feminine wile, a reminder that she occupies a world where such teasing is the height of dramatic tension. This arch quality will be utterly dismantled within moments, when the photograph reveals her own cruelty. Priestley uses the word to mark the distance between the trivial self she presents and the moral self the Inspector will excavate; the arch glance at Gerald is, in retrospect, a glance into a mirror that is about to shatter.
ex_en: Sheila's arch remark about her shopping trip was the last flicker of the spoiled, self-absorbed girl she had been for twenty-three years, extinguished the instant she saw Eva Smith's face.
ex_zh: Archly, she tilted her head and aimed the arrow of her smile at Gerald, not noticing that the bow was already splintering in her hands.

def_en: clumsy, overbearing, or oppressive in manner; lacking subtlety or tact
def_zh: a bit heavy-handed, getting heavy-handed, heavy-handed approach
quote_en: "Gerald: Getting a bit heavy- handed, aren't you, inspector?" – After the Inspector insists that Eric must stay and be questioned, Gerald steps in with a complaint about the Inspector's oppressive, tactless method (Act 1)
quote_zh: Gerald's accusation of 'heavy-handedness' is a gentleman's protest against a method that refuses to follow the rules of polite interrogation. The compound adjective suggests a lack of finesse, a bullying pressure that offends Gerald's sense of fair play. Priestley gives Gerald this line to expose the limits of his moral imagination: for Gerald, the real offence is not the suffering of Eva Smith but the Inspector's failure to treat the family with the deference owed to their station. The word is also deeply ironic, for Gerald himself will later be revealed as someone who used a heavy hand of a different kind—the hand that installed a desperate girl in a flat for his own convenience. The adjective thus marks the point where gentlemanly complaint shades into moral evasion.
ex_en: Gerald's charge that the Inspector was heavy-handed was the protest of a man who preferred his truths served with a silver spoon, not carved out with the blunt knife the Inspector wielded.
ex_zh: Heavy-handed, Gerald called him, as if the weight of his own guilt were a feather and the Inspector's insistence on weighing it were the real crime.

def_en: a dishonest or unprincipled man; a scoundrel who habitually cheats or exploits others
def_zh: worst rogues, scoundrel and rogue, charming rogue
quote_en: "Gerald: He's a notorious womanizer as well as being one of the worst sots and rogues in Brumley-" – Gerald, identifying Alderman Meggarty, adds 'rogue' to the alderman's catalogue of vices, portraying him as fundamentally dishonest (Act 2)
quote_zh: 'Rogue' completes the trinity of vices Gerald assigns to Meggarty: womanizer, sot, rogue. The word carries an almost affectionate contempt in some contexts—a 'loveable rogue'—but Gerald's tone strips it of charm. A rogue is a cheat, a swindler, a man who operates outside the rules that bind decent society. Priestley uses the noun to suggest that Meggarty's corruption is not merely private but structural: he is a 'rogue' who has, somehow, remained in public office. The word thus indicts the entire civic system that protects such men. And as before, the accusation rebounds: Gerald's own behaviour with Daisy Renton, though smoother and better-mannered, was the act of a man who used his money to secure what he wanted and then walked away—a rogue in a dinner jacket.
ex_en: Alderman Meggarty, the 'rogue' Gerald condemned so freely, was only the most visible symptom of a disease that infected every alderman and magistrate who valued profit and pleasure above human life.
ex_zh: The rogue Meggarty waddled through the town hall corridors, his dishonesty as comfortable on him as a well-worn coat, its pockets lined with the silence of those who knew better.

def_en: a mixture of various ingredients, especially in a drink; often used dismissively to describe something improvised
def_zh: some such concoction, strange concoction, sweet concoction
quote_en: "Gerald: I went along to the County Hotel, which I knew would be quiet at that time of night, and we had a drink or two and talked. Inspector: did she drink much at the time? Gerald: no. she only had a port and lemonade - or some such concoction." – Gerald describes his first evening with Daisy Renton, and the Inspector probes her state; Gerald's reply inadvertently reveals how young and unsophisticated she was (Act 2)
quote_zh: The word 'concoction' is a tiny, careless detail that speaks volumes. Gerald's dismissive tone—'or some such concoction'—frames Daisy's drink as a naive, almost childish mixture, the sort of thing a girl who does not know how to drink would order. The noun distances Gerald from her: he is a man of the world who can name his spirits; she is a girl who sips at something sweet and improvised. Priestley uses this small moment to underline the power imbalance in their relationship. The concoction is a marker of Daisy's innocence, and Gerald's recollection of it, in front of Sheila, is a casual betrayal of how little he respected the woman he set up as his mistress. The drink, mixed and unpretentious, becomes an emblem of the life he casually consumed.
ex_en: Gerald's mention of Daisy's 'concoction' was an unintentional confession: he had taken a girl who still drank like a child and made her the temporary centre of an adult world he had no intention of letting her keep.
ex_zh: The port and lemonade, that sweet concoction, sat between them like the whole of Daisy's future: mixed on the spur of the moment, briefly sparkling, and then forgotten on the table when the gentleman paid the bill.

def_en: intensely irritating and frustrating; causing a loss of patience
def_zh: damned exasperating, most exasperating, find it exasperating
quote_en: "Birling: Well, my dear, they're so damned exasperating. They just won't try to understand our position or to see the difference between a lot of stuff like this coming out in private and a downright public scandal." – After the Inspector's departure and the revelation that he was not a real policeman, Birling fumes at Sheila and Eric for their refusal to sweep the evening's events under the carpet (Act 3)
quote_zh: 'Exasperating' is Birling's final, exhausted judgment on his children's morality. The adjective conveys a parental frustration that is entirely misplaced: Birling is exasperated not by the cruelty his family has committed but by their stubborn insistence on taking it seriously. To him, Sheila and Eric's refusal to 'behave sensibly' is a form of disobedience, a failure to protect the family's reputation. Priestley uses this word to show that Birling has learned nothing: even after confessing his own actions, he still views the whole affair as a matter of public relations rather than moral reckoning. The exasperation he directs at his children is the last gasp of a man whose entire worldview is being rejected by the very people he once controlled.
ex_en: Birling's exasperation with his children was the clearest sign that he had understood nothing of the evening's lesson; he was annoyed not at the sin but at the refusal to pretend it had not been committed.
ex_zh: Exasperating as a fly that would not be swatted, Sheila and Eric's moral stubbornness buzzed around Birling's ears, a persistent, infuriating hum that no amount of port could silence.

def_en: relating to or characteristic of a residential district on the outskirts of a city, often connoting middle-class conventionality
def_zh: suburban house, suburban street, suburban life
quote_en: "The dining room is of a fairly large suburban house, belonging to a prosperous manufacturer." – The opening stage directions that establish the Birlings' home as a comfortable but unremarkable product of middle-class aspiration on the edge of an industrial city (Act 1)
quote_zh: Priestley's use of 'suburban' in the very first description of the Birlings' world immediately pins the family to a specific social class: they are neither the old landed gentry nor the urban working poor, but the in-between class whose values are defined by respectability and material comfort. The word carries a faint sneer in the context of 1945, when audiences had begun to associate suburbia with a certain spiritual timidity—a retreat from the real, gritty life of the city into a safe, manicured insularity. By situating the play in a 'suburban house', Priestley signals that the Birlings have deliberately distanced themselves from the consequences of the industrial capitalism that made them rich. The Inspector's arrival will collapse that distance, forcing the city's misery through the suburban front door.
ex_en: The Birlings' suburban house, with its 'good solid furniture', was a fortress built to keep out the sight and sound of the factories that paid for it.
ex_zh: Suburbia stretched its neat hedges around the Birling home like a blindfold, so perfectly tied that no one inside could see the smoke rising from the chimneys where their fortune was forged.

def_en: financially successful; wealthy and thriving
def_zh: prosperous manufacturer, prosperous family, prosperous times
quote_en: "The dining room is of a fairly large suburban house, belonging to a prosperous manufacturer." – The opening stage directions, pinpointing the economic source of the Birlings' comfort as manufacturing profit (Act 1)
quote_zh: 'Prosperous' is the economic label that defines Birling's entire identity. Priestley chooses the word deliberately over synonyms like 'wealthy' or 'rich' because 'prosperous' carries a moral connotation of success earned through virtue and hard work, a myth Birling subscribes to entirely. The audience, however, will soon learn that Birling's prosperity is built on the labour of women like Eva Smith, whom he paid 'twenty-two and six' a week and dismissed for asking for a tiny fraction more. The word therefore becomes an indictment: this 'prosperous manufacturer' is prosperous precisely because he refuses to share prosperity with those who create it. The adjective's positive shine tarnishes as the play proceeds, until by the end it sounds like a accusation.
ex_en: The 'prosperous' Birling household, glittering with champagne and silver, was a monument to the principle that one man's fortune is often built on the carefully managed poverty of hundreds of unseen workers.
ex_zh: Prosperous as a well-fed cat, the Birling house sat purring on its street corner, its whiskers twitching only when a stranger's knock disturbed the cream.

def_en: a female domestic servant employed to wait at table, answer the door, and maintain the parlour
def_zh: the parlourmaid, a parlourmaid's duties, parlourmaid Edna
quote_en: "EDNA, the parlourmaid, is just clearing the table, which has no cloth, of the dessert plates and champagne glasses, etc, and then replacing them with decanter of port, cigar box and cigarettes." – The stage directions as the curtain rises, showing the maid Edna performing the invisible labour that sustains the Birlings' celebration (Act 1)
quote_zh: 'Parlourmaid' is a word that defines Edna entirely by her function. She has no surname in the character list, no lines beyond 'Yes, ma'am' and the announcement of the Inspector's arrival, and she exits the play almost immediately, never to return. Priestley includes her in the opening tableau to remind the audience that the Birlings' comfort depends on the silent, invisible labour of servants like Edna, who are themselves only a small step removed from the factory girls the family exploits. The parlourmaid clears the plates and pours the port, her presence a living symbol of the class system that the play dissects. That she is the one to announce the Inspector—ushering truth into the room—makes her, momentarily, the most important person in the drama.
ex_en: Edna the parlourmaid, clearing the table and answering the door, was the silent machinery that kept the Birling household turning, as invisible to the family as the grease on the factory floor.
ex_zh: The parlourmaid moved through the room like a ghost in a starched apron, her presence noted only when a glass needed filling or a door needed opening, and forgotten the instant her task was done.

def_en: in a manner that shows open resistance or bold disobedience
def_zh: answer defiantly, say defiantly, stand defiantly
quote_en: "Birling: (sharply, staring at him) what's the matter with you? Eric: (defiantly) Nothing." – After Gerald jokes that the Inspector might have come about something Eric has done, Birling notices his son's unease and snaps a question; Eric's reply is a sullen, unconvincing denial (Act 1)
quote_zh: The stage direction 'defiantly' captures the entire dynamic between Eric and his father in a single adverb. Eric's 'Nothing' is not an answer but a refusal to answer, a sullen barricade thrown up against paternal authority. The defiance is adolescent and clumsy, and Birling's sharp stare suggests he knows it. Priestley uses this small exchange to foreshadow the far more damaging defiance to come: Eric's secret drinking, his theft from the company, and his desperate refusal to confess until the Inspector leaves him no choice. The adverb reveals a young man at war with a father who commands but does not listen, and it goes a long way toward explaining why, when Eric found himself 'in a hell of a state' about Eva's pregnancy, he could not bring himself to go to the man now glaring at him across the port.
ex_en: Eric's defiant 'Nothing' was a locked door behind which he hid his guilt from a father who had never given him any reason to believe the door could safely be opened.
ex_zh: Defiantly, Eric flung the word 'Nothing' at his father like a pebble at a tank, the small, brave clink of resistance swallowed instantly by the armour of Birling's certainty.

def_en: the state of being irritated, bothered, or slightly angry
def_zh: show annoyance, express annoyance, considerable annoyance
quote_en: "Gerald: (showing annoyance) Any particular reason why I shouldn't see this girl's photograph, inspector? Inspector: (coolly, looking hard at him) There might be." – The Inspector shows Birling the photograph of Eva Smith but blocks both Gerald and Eric from seeing it; Gerald, accustomed to deference, bristles at being excluded (Act 1)
quote_zh: Gerald's 'annoyance' is the first flicker of the gentleman's entitlement in the face of a working man who refuses to be managed. The noun is mild—this is not fury or outrage—but it reveals Gerald's expectation that he should be treated as an equal by anyone, even a police inspector. Priestley uses this small emotional signal to distinguish Gerald's reaction from Birling's bluster: Gerald does not threaten or bluster; he is simply 'annoyed', in the way a customer might be annoyed by a waiter's slowness. The Inspector's cool reply—'There might be'—is a masterclass in withholding, and Gerald's annoyance marks the precise point where the play's class politics begin to bite. The man who is used to getting answers has just been told he is not entitled to them.
ex_en: Gerald's annoyance at being denied the photograph was the reflex of a man who had never been told 'no' by anyone he considered his social inferior.
ex_zh: Annoyance fluttered across Gerald's face like a shadow of a passing cloud, brief and superficial, not yet the storm of guilt that was gathering on the horizon.

def_en: unable to keep still or stay settled; showing signs of nervous energy or impatience
def_zh: move restlessly, grow restless, restless night
quote_en: "Birling: I see. Sensible really. (moves restlessly, then turns.) you've had enough of that port, Eric. // the inspector is watching Birling and now Birling notices him.//" – After the Inspector explains his method of 'one person and one line of inquiry at a time', Birling, trying to regain control, fidgets and redirects attention to his son (Act 1)
quote_zh: The stage direction 'moves restlessly' is a tiny piece of physical theatre that betrays Birling's crumbling authority. Up to this point he has faced the Inspector with bluster and name-dropping, but the Inspector's calm refusal to be managed has rattled him. The restless movement signals that Birling's body knows what his mind will not admit: he is losing control. His sudden snap at Eric—'you've had enough of that port'—is a displaced attempt to reassert dominance over the one person in the room he can still command. Priestley uses 'restlessly' to show that the Inspector's presence is working like a slow acid on the family's composure, dissolving the outer layers of politeness to reveal the raw nerves beneath.
ex_en: Birling's restless pacing was the first physical admission that the Inspector had unsettled him, the body confessing what the mouth still denied.
ex_zh: Restlessness crept over Birling like an itch he could not scratch, his fingers drumming on the back of a chair in a rhythm that spelled out the word he would not say: 'guilty'.

def_en: giving cause for serious thought or alarm; solemn and serious in manner
def_zh: grave voice, grave expression, look grave
quote_en: "Birling: Yes. Incidentally we've been modestly celebrating his engagement to my daughter, Sheila. Inspector: I see. Mr Croft is going to marry Miss Sheila Birling? Gerald: (smiling) I hope so. Inspector: (gravely) Then I'd prefer you to stay." – When Birling, trying to dismiss the gravity of the inquiry, mentions the engagement, the Inspector's response transforms the occasion from a celebration into a sombre moral reckoning (Act 1)
quote_zh: The stage direction 'gravely' switches the emotional register of the scene in an instant. Gerald's smiling 'I hope so' belongs to the world of champagne toasts and engagement rings, but the Inspector's grave reply—'Then I'd prefer you to stay'—drops the temperature like a sudden frost. The word 'gravely' carries connotations of death, of the grave itself, and it silently connects the engagement celebration to the corpse in the infirmary. Priestley uses this single adverb to signal that the Inspector is not investigating a routine matter but conducting something closer to an inquest, and that no amount of social pleasantry will lighten the proceedings. Gerald's smile dies on his face, and the audience understands that the engagement, like the port, has just been poisoned.
ex_en: The Inspector's grave tone, after Gerald's light-hearted engagement confirmation, was a quiet tolling of a bell that no one in the room yet knew was ringing for them.
ex_zh: Gravely, the Inspector accepted the invitation to the family table, and the word fell into the champagne like a drop of ink, blackening everything it touched.

def_en: an idea, belief, or conception, especially one that is vague or fanciful
def_zh: have a notion, strange notion, dismiss the notion
quote_en: "Sheila: But these girls aren't cheap labour - they're people. Inspector: (dryly) I've had that notion myself from time to time. In fact, I've thought that it would do us all a bit of good if sometimes we tried to put ourselves in the place of these young women counting their pennies, in their dingy little back bedrooms." – When Birling and the Inspector discuss the economics of cheap labour, Sheila blurts out a truth that her father cannot see, and the Inspector endorses it with a quiet, ironic understatement (Act 1)
quote_zh: 'Notion' is the word the Inspector uses to describe the radical idea that working-class women are human beings. By calling it a 'notion', he borrows the dismissive language of the Birlings' world—where ideas like collective responsibility are dismissed as 'cranks' and 'nonsense'—and turns it back on them. The dryness of his delivery ('dryly') adds a layer of irony: the idea that 'these girls aren't cheap labour - they're people' is so obvious that it should not be a 'notion' at all but a given. Priestley uses this small, almost throwaway exchange to crystallise the play's central moral argument and to align the Inspector with Sheila, who has just begun to see what her father cannot.
ex_en: Sheila's sudden notion that Eva Smith was a person, not a cost, was the first green shoot of her moral awakening, and the Inspector's quiet agreement watered it into something that would grow long after he left.
ex_zh: The notion that the poor might have souls was, in the Birling household, as novel and unsettling as a stranger walking uninvited into a private room and refusing to leave.

def_en: a sharp, angry, or witty reply, especially one that turns the words of the previous speaker back on them
def_zh: make a retort, sharp retort, witty retort
quote_en: "Birling looks as if about to make some retort, then thinks better of it, and goes out, closing door sharply behind him." – After the Inspector rebukes Birling for his priorities by comparing Eva Smith's 'nasty mess' of a death to the 'nasty mess' Birling accuses him of making of the evening, Birling is silenced and, for once, has no words (Act 1)
quote_zh: The stage direction 'retort' marks a rare and significant moment: Birling, who has spent the entire scene blustering, interrupting, and threatening, is stopped in his tracks. A retort is a verbal counter-punch, and Birling's instinct is to deliver one, but 'thinks better of it' signals that the Inspector has landed a blow too powerful to parry. Priestley uses this small physical moment to dramatise the first real victory of the Inspector's moral authority over Birling's social authority. The contrast between Eva's 'nasty mess'—her burnt-out body in the infirmary—and the 'nasty mess' Birling accuses the Inspector of making of his dinner party is so devastating that Birling can only retreat, slamming the door behind him like a child sent from the room.
ex_en: Birling's unspoken retort was the first admission that the Inspector had said something unanswerable, a truth so sharp that even a man armed with knighthoods and aldermanic titles could not deflect it.
ex_zh: The retort died on Birling's lips like a struck match that refuses to flare, its tiny wisp of smoke the only trace of the fire that had roared there a moment before.

def_en: feeling sudden shock, alarm, or surprise, often accompanied by a physical flinch or gasp
def_zh: look startled, sound startled, visibly startled
quote_en: "Inspector: So first she changed her name to Daisy Renton. Gerald: (startled) What? Inspector: (steadily) I said she changed her name to Daisy Renton." – As the Inspector moves from Sheila's confession to the next phase of Eva's life, he drops the name 'Daisy Renton', and Gerald's involuntary reaction betrays his connection before he can control it (Act 1)
quote_zh: The stage direction 'startled' is the single word that convicts Gerald. Up to this point, Gerald has presented himself as a calm, detached observer, a gentleman above the messy emotions of the inquiry. But the name 'Daisy Renton' hits him like a physical blow, and his startled 'What?'—the first unguarded sound he has made all evening—is a confession in a single syllable. Priestley uses this tiny, involuntary reaction to demonstrate the power of the truth to shatter even the most polished composure. The Inspector, who has been waiting for this moment, simply repeats the name 'steadily', confirming that the trap has sprung. Gerald's startled reflex is the hinge on which the entire second act will turn.
ex_en: Gerald's startled exclamation at the name 'Daisy Renton' betrayed in an instant the secret he had kept from Sheila all summer, a secret that now lay exposed on the dining table like a dead flower.
ex_zh: Startled as a bird from a hedge, Gerald's composure flew up and scattered, leaving him blinking and exposed in the sudden, harsh light that the Inspector had been waiting to turn on.

def_en: a glass vessel, typically with a stopper, into which wine or spirits are decanted before serving
def_zh: crystal decanter, decanter of port, decanter and glasses
quote_en: "EDNA, the parlourmaid, is just clearing the table, which has no cloth, of the dessert plates and champagne glasses, etc, and then replacing them with decanter of port, cigar box and cigarettes." – The opening stage directions, establishing the ritual of masculine authority and the material comforts that define the Birlings' world before the Inspector's entrance (Act 1)
quote_zh: Priestley's 'decanter' is a small but potent symbol of the Edwardian social order. The ritual of Edna placing it on the table—after the women have left the room—marks the port as exclusively masculine property, a symbol of the patriarchal control that Birling and Gerald will later use to justify their treatment of Eva Smith. The decanter is elegant, heavy, and expensive, and its presence reinforces the prosperity that Birling so proudly claims. Yet the port inside it, smooth and intoxicating, is also the substance that will loosen Eric's tongue and reveal his drunkenness. Priestley thus loads an ordinary domestic object with multiple meanings: celebration, exclusion, hypocrisy, and the false comfort that will be shattered by the Inspector's arrival.
ex_en: The decanter of port, gleaming on the dining table like a promise of endless prosperity, was the first object the Inspector's gaze fell upon, and the first to seem suddenly obscene against the image of a dead girl in a pauper's ward.
ex_zh: The decanter sat among them like a ruby-hearted lie, its cut glass facets throwing tiny rainbows onto the white linen while the real world, the world of strikes and suicide, remained outside in the dark.

def_en: firm and stable in shape; not liquid or gas; of dependable and substantial quality
def_zh: solid furniture, solid silver, solid and substantial
quote_en: "It has a good solid furniture of the period." – The opening stage directions, describing the dining room of the Birlings' suburban house (Act 1)
quote_zh: Priestley's use of 'solid' to describe the Birlings' furniture is a deliberate piece of characterisation through set design. The adjective suggests permanence, weight, and prosperous respectability—qualities that Arthur Birling believes define his own character. But the word also carries a hint of what the furniture is not: it is not delicate, not graceful, not refined. 'Solid' betrays the Birlings' world as one of material accumulation rather than aesthetic or moral sensibility. As the play unfolds, solidity itself becomes a lie: the family's certainties, like the 'unsinkable' Titanic Birling mentions, will prove spectacularly fragile. The solid furniture becomes a visual irony, a heaviness that cannot anchor the family against the tide of guilt the Inspector unleashes.
ex_en: The 'solid furniture' of the Birlings' dining room was a monument to Arthur Birling's belief that the world was as dependable as his bank balance, a belief that the Inspector would shatter without moving a single chair.
ex_zh: Solid as a vault door, the mahogany sideboard stood guard over the family's secrets, not knowing that the flood was already seeping under its polished feet.

def_en: in a way that is humble, unassuming, or not ostentatious; without showiness
def_zh: modestly celebrating, modestly priced, speak modestly
quote_en: "Birling: Yes. Incidentally we've been modestly celebrating his engagement to my daughter, Sheila." – Birling introduces Gerald and the occasion to the Inspector, downplaying the celebration while simultaneously drawing attention to his own restraint (Act 1)
quote_zh: Birling's 'modestly' is a mask that fools no one, least of all the Inspector. The adverb is an attempt to appear unassuming while the champagne glasses are still being cleared from the table and the port decanter sits heavy with promise. 'Modesty' in Birling's vocabulary does not mean genuine humility—it is the performance of humility by a man who believes he has earned the right to be immodest. Priestley uses this word to expose Birling's social insecurity: the truly secure aristocrat does not need to announce that his celebration is modest; he simply enjoys it. The word thus becomes another entry in the catalogue of Birling's pretensions, and it makes his later bluster about knighthoods and chief constables all the more ridiculous.
ex_en: Birling's claim of a 'modest' celebration was one more piece of stagecraft in the long performance of a man who had spent his life trying to look as though he already belonged to the class above him.
ex_zh: Modestly, he gestured at the champagne, the silver, the array of crystal, as if to suggest that on any ordinary night the family dined on bread and water and counted themselves fortunate.

def_en: in a manner that indicates doubt, hesitation, or uncertainty
def_zh: look dubiously, ask dubiously, reply dubiously
quote_en: "Sheila: (staring at him) yes. That's true. You know. (she goes close to him, wonderingly.) I don't understand about you. Inspector: (calmly) there's no reason why you should. // he regards her calmly while she stares at him wonderingly and dubiously. //" – After the Inspector's comment about sharing guilt, Sheila approaches him with a mixture of awe and suspicion, the stage direction capturing her inability to fully grasp his nature (Act 2)
quote_zh: The stage direction 'dubiously' captures Sheila's evolving relationship with the Inspector. By Act 2, she has moved from tearful confession to a state of alert, questioning attention; she senses that the Inspector is not an ordinary policeman, and her dubiety marks the beginning of her independent moral intelligence. Priestley uses this adverb to signal that Sheila is now a co-investigator, not merely a confessor. The word also hints at the supernatural or allegorical dimension of the Inspector—his calm 'there's no reason why you should' acknowledges the mystery without resolving it. Sheila's dubiety is the audience's own, suspended between accepting the Inspector as a realist character and understanding him as something more: a moral force, a collective conscience, or a prophet.
ex_en: Sheila's dubiety, as she stared at the Inspector, was the first moment she became the audience's stand-in, asking the question that everyone in the theatre was thinking but could not articulate.
ex_zh: Dubiously, she examined the man who had dismantled her family with nothing more than a photograph and a voice, as if searching for the seam in a magic trick that had somehow, impossibly, drawn real blood.

def_en: to assert the opposite of a statement made by someone; to deny the truth of
def_zh: contradict me, don't contradict, openly contradict
quote_en: "Mrs Birling: please don't contradict me like that." – After Sheila warns her mother not to build a wall between the family and the dead girl, Mrs Birling rebukes her daughter for challenging her authority (Act 2)
quote_zh: 'Contradict' is a word that reveals Mrs Birling's understanding of family hierarchy as inviolable. To her, Sheila's disagreement is not a moral intervention but a breach of deference—a daughter's duty is to assent, not to 'contradict'. Priestley places this verb in the middle of a scene where Sheila is, for the first time, speaking truth to power within her own family, and her mother's response is to silence her on procedural grounds. The word thus exposes the Birlings' household as a miniature tyranny where truth is less important than obedience. The irony is that Mrs Birling will later contradict herself so thoroughly—condemning the unknown father of Eva's child while unknowingly describing Eric—that the verb will return to haunt her.
ex_en: Mrs Birling's command not to contradict her was the voice of an old order that could not tolerate dissent, even when the dissent came from a daughter who had just learned the cost of silence.
ex_zh: Contradict me, she said, and the word fell like a portcullis between mother and daughter, a barrier that the Inspector's quiet voice was already levering open from the other side.

def_en: to express sharp disapproval or criticism of someone because of their behaviour or actions
def_zh: sharply rebuke, rebuke them, rebuke a child
quote_en: "Mrs Birling: (rebuking them) I'm talking to the inspector now, if you don't mind." – When Gerald and Sheila try to intervene during Mrs Birling's interrogation, she silences them with a sharp, maternal correction (Act 2)
quote_zh: Mrs Birling's 'rebuke' is a display of social dominance masked as parental discipline. The verb suggests the correction of a child, and by applying it to both her daughter and her future son-in-law, Mrs Birling asserts her position at the top of a domestic hierarchy she believes is unshakeable. Priestley uses this small moment to illustrate how the older generation uses the language of authority to avoid being questioned. Yet the rebuke is also a sign of weakness: Mrs Birling silences the others precisely because she senses, however dimly, that what they might say could undermine her. The word marks the precise point where her composure begins to crack, a crack that will widen into the collapse of her entire moral self-image.
ex_en: Mrs Birling's rebuke of her daughter and Gerald was the last time she would speak with such unthinking confidence; within minutes, the ground beneath her would open and the voice she had silenced would be the only one that mattered.
ex_zh: She rebuked them with the practised ease of a headmistress who has never been questioned, her voice a ruler cracked across the knuckles of the only people who might have saved her.

def_en: causing someone to feel deeply hurt, upset, or angry; unpleasant or disagreeable to the senses
def_zh: offensive manner, deeply offensive, find it offensive
quote_en: "Mrs Birling: I realize that you may have to conduct some sort of inquiry, but I must say that so far you seem to be conducting it in a rather peculiar and offensive manner." – Mrs Birling, early in Act 2, attempts to assert her social authority over the Inspector by criticising his method as rude and inappropriate (Act 2)
quote_zh: The word 'offensive' is Mrs Birling's most characteristic weapon. She is not offended by the death of Eva Smith, nor by her son's alcoholism, nor by her daughter's cruelty—but she is deeply offended by the Inspector's failure to show her the deference she believes she is owed. Priestley uses this adjective to expose how thoroughly the Birlings' moral vocabulary has been corrupted: 'offensive' describes not cruelty but discourtesy, not the destruction of a life but the breach of a social code. The word also reveals Mrs Birling's complete incomprehension of what the Inspector is actually doing. She thinks she is being subjected to a rude interrogation; she is actually being offered a chance at moral redemption, and she will reject it with every syllable of offended dignity she can summon.
ex_en: Mrs Birling's complaint that the Inspector was 'offensive' was the clearest possible sign that she had no moral categories beyond those of the drawing room: rudeness was the worst sin she could imagine.
ex_zh: Offensive—the word rose from her like a scent of wilting flowers, a signal that her sensibilities had been bruised, while the body of a girl she had refused to help was still being washed in the infirmary.

def_en: unable to be upset or excited; calm and composed, especially under provocation
def_zh: imperturbable calm, imperturbable manner, remain imperturbable
quote_en: "Inspector: (imperturbable) Yes. Now what about Mr Birling?" – After Mrs Birling has called his manner 'a trifle impertinent' and tried to silence Sheila, the Inspector remains utterly unruffled, his calm a rebuke in itself (Act 2)
quote_zh: The stage direction 'imperturbable' is the Inspector's secret weapon. While Mrs Birling grows increasingly heated, the Inspector maintains a composure so steady that it becomes more intimidating than any outburst. The word derives from the Latin *perturbare* (to disturb greatly), and the prefix *im-* negates it entirely: he is the man who cannot be disturbed. Priestley uses this quality to present the Inspector as something more than human—a force of moral gravity that the Birlings' social weaponry cannot touch. His imperturbability is the stillness at the centre of the storm, and it contrasts starkly with the family's escalating agitation. The word also suggests that the Inspector has heard every objection before and knows, with terrible certainty, exactly how the evening will end.
ex_en: The Inspector's imperturbable calm was the fixed point around which the Birlings' panic whirled, a stillness that was more frightening than any threat.
ex_zh: Imperturbable as a deep river under ice, the Inspector received every insult and evasion not as an obstacle but as a confirmation that the current beneath was already carrying him to his destination.

def_en: to succeed in persuading or leading someone to do something; to bring about or give rise to
def_zh: nothing would induce, induce her to, induce someone to go
quote_en: "Sheila: nothing would induce me. I want to understand exactly what happens when a man says he's so busy at the works that he can hardly ever find time to come and see the girl he's supposed to be in love with." – After Gerald begs Sheila to leave the room and spare herself the details of his confession, she refuses absolutely, her determination now fixed on learning the whole truth (Act 2)
quote_zh: 'Induce' is the pivot on which Sheila's moral transformation turns. The verb suggests persuasion, enticement—the kind of gentle pressure that has governed Sheila's life as a protected, upper-middle-class daughter. By declaring that 'nothing would induce me', Sheila announces her emancipation from the world of social convenience. She will not be coaxed, bribed, or protected from the truth. Priestley uses this moment to show that Sheila has learned the Inspector's central lesson: that comfort purchased by ignorance is a form of complicity. The word also marks the definitive end of her engagement as it existed before; the girl who could be induced to smile and look away no longer exists.
ex_en: Sheila's refusal to be induced away from the truth was the moment she ceased to be Gerald's fiancée in spirit, even if she still wore his ring on her finger.
ex_zh: Nothing could induce her, not the promise of restored peace nor the pleading in his eyes, to turn back now from the path she had begun to walk the instant she recognised the photograph.

def_en: approaches made with the intention of initiating a sexual or romantic relationship, especially when unwelcome
def_zh: sexual advances, make advances, repel advances
quote_en: "Gerald: She told me that Joe Meggarty's advances had left her rather shaken - as well they might- -" – Gerald describes Daisy Renton's account of the drunken alderman's predatory behaviour in the Palace Bar, which prompted Gerald to intervene (Act 2)
quote_zh: The noun 'advances' is a euphemism that does not soften the reality of what it describes. Gerald uses the word to recount Meggarty's behaviour, but Priestley ensures the audience hears the violence beneath the polite term: Meggarty 'wedged her into a corner with that obscene fat carcass of his', and his 'advances' were unmistakably coercive. The word belongs to the language of courtship and gentility, yet its application here reveals how the respectable classes concealed sexual predation behind a thin veneer of social language. The irony is doubled: Gerald himself made 'advances' to Daisy Renton, albeit with better manners, and the word thus links the gentleman and the alderman in a single, uncomfortable chain of male entitlement.
ex_en: Gerald's mention of Meggarty's 'advances' was an unwitting description of his own actions, dressed in a better suit and delivered with a polished accent.
ex_zh: Advances, he called them, as if the alderman had offered her a dance card at a ball rather than pressing her into a corner with his stinking breath and his fat, uninvited hands.
